Madam-President, I believe there is broad agreement that under these extraordinary circumstances, characterized by Commissioner Genti-Lon's recent description, we require robust governance—both economic and social. Essential for resilience and sustainability, this governance must encompass both fiscal measures and social programs. While the immediate focus should be on stabilization efforts, acknowledging potential impacts on growth and employment remains crucial. Given the current backdrop, addressing labor market issues becomes imperative. Thus, the parliamentary term serves as an effective mechanism to ensure comprehensive governance.

Governance must address multiple fronts: fiscal measures, social initiatives, and economic policy coordination. Addressing societal stability alongside economic recovery is vital, particularly in light of the refugee influx. Recognizing the unique vulnerability faced by refugees, a coordinated approach involving rapid and substantial financial assistance from the EU is necessary. Implementing the proposed 'Cohesion Action for Refugees in Europe' (CURE) initiative underscores this necessity.

Furthermore, ensuring that every child receives adequate support is paramount. Children orphaned by conflict represent a significant concern, requiring targeted interventions to prevent their loss to generational neglect. This commitment extends beyond children; it includes the educational needs of adults affected by displacement, reflecting the collective responsibility towards future generations.

Addressing the economic fallout of the ongoing war requires innovative strategies tailored to the specific vulnerabilities within the EU. Investments in social welfare systems and targeted aid programs are essential to mitigate adverse effects and build sustainable futures for displaced individuals and communities across the bloc.

Madam President, I think that there is a large consensus that, in this exceptional situation, described by Commissioner Gentiloni just now, we need a strong semester. We need strong governance – economic, social – in the European Union. We need, obviously, strong solidarity and we have managed, up to now, to show this strong solidarity. I think we have learned a lesson from the COVID crisis that a strong economy can only be a resilient economy, an economy that adapts rapidly and that also creates the resources to respond to challenges. The big challenge we have now is certainly how we can stabilise the recovery. There will certainly be an impact on growth. There will be, or there might be, an impact on employment. Some weeks ago, we noticed that it was about job shortages, qualified labour shortages. Now, maybe, given the situation and given the increases in prices of energy and of food, we might again head for more difficulties on our labour markets. So we have to be ready to respond to that. Therefore, the semester is an essential tool to have strong governance. This governance has to be economic, certainly, fiscal, yes, but also social. These are the two sides of the same coin if we want a resilient economy and a resilient society. It has been said that we now also need stability in our society. During the COVID crisis, the most vulnerable paid a high price and we have to avoid the price of this terrible war being borne by the most vulnerable. First, on refugees, I think here there is a need for very, very strong and rapid solidarity. We do not know the numbers. It has been said that it’s about two million today and there might be seven, eight or more million people who have to be received and welcomed, and who have to get all the support they need, especially in those Member States who are the ‘frontline’ Member States. The Commission yesterday adopted a package, as a first step, with the Asylum, Migration and Integration Fund, of EUR 400 million, and with REACT-EU, which can make available about EUR 10 billion which, in a very flexible way, can be devoted to taking care of the people arriving. We have a proposal on a new system, which is similar to the coronavirus response investment initiative (CRII) system, and which we call ‘cohesion’s action for refugees in Europe’ (CARE), which also allows Member States to take funds from the Cohesion Fund to allocate to the reception of refugees. This is a duty, and there is a particular duty, because you have, Mr Geuking, spoken – rightly so, by the way – on children. There are, among the refugees, hundreds of thousands of children who have lost everything. And there’s a duty now to take care of these children, as it is a duty, by the way, to take care of all children. I fully agree with you. Therefore, in a few days, we will have the national plans on the implementation of the child guarantee and we also have to include in this child guarantee the children of refugees. It is not conceivable that these children have no education, that they will be nowhere, that they will be the lost Ukrainian generation, which is, by the way, the aim of the dictator in Moscow. Therefore, we have a strong responsibility to give them – again, as for all children – the right support, the right education, the right care. Yes, this war is a challenge for poverty. In the European Union, we still have a high degree of poverty. More than 96 million people are at risk of poverty. We have taken an important commitment by reducing it by at least 15 million, including at least 5 million children, and it cannot be that the war will increase poverty in Europe. This is a challenge. We have to find the right responses, the right resources, the right policies and the right investments in our economies, but also in our social systems.

We do not know the numbers. So we have to be ready to respond to that. In the European Union, we still have a high degree of poverty. This is a challenge. We need strong governance – economic, social – in the European Union. It has been said that we now also need stability in our society. Therefore, we have a strong responsibility to give them – again, as for all children – the right support, the right education, the right care. Therefore, in a few days, we will have the national plans on the implementation of the child guarantee and we also have to include in this child guarantee the children of refugees. Madam President, I think that there is a large consensus that, in this exceptional situation, described by Commissioner Gentiloni just now, we need a strong semester. More than 96 million people are at risk of poverty. The big challenge we have now is certainly how we can stabilise the recovery. During the COVID crisis, the most vulnerable paid a high price and we have to avoid the price of this terrible war being borne by the most vulnerable. These are the two sides of the same coin if we want a resilient economy and a resilient society. Therefore, the semester is an essential tool to have strong governance. We have taken an important commitment by reducing it by at least 15 million, including at least 5 million children, and it cannot be that the war will increase poverty in Europe. The Commission yesterday adopted a package, as a first step, with the Asylum, Migration and Integration Fund, of EUR 400 million, and with REACT-EU, which can make available about EUR 10 billion which, in a very flexible way, can be devoted to taking care of the people arriving. There will be, or there might be, an impact on employment. Yes, this war is a challenge for poverty. I think we have learned a lesson from the COVID crisis that a strong economy can only be a resilient economy, an economy that adapts rapidly and that also creates the resources to respond to challenges. There will certainly be an impact on growth. First, on refugees, I think here there is a need for very, very strong and rapid solidarity. There are, among the refugees, hundreds of thousands of children who have lost everything. Now, maybe, given the situation and given the increases in prices of energy and of food, we might again head for more difficulties on our labour markets. This governance has to be economic, certainly, fiscal, yes, but also social. This is a duty, and there is a particular duty, because you have, Mr Geuking, spoken – rightly so, by the way – on children. And there’s a duty now to take care of these children, as it is a duty, by the way, to take care of all children. Some weeks ago, we noticed that it was about job shortages, qualified labour shortages. We have a proposal on a new system, which is similar to the coronavirus response investment initiative (CRII) system, and which we call ‘cohesion’s action for refugees in Europe’ (CARE), which also allows Member States to take funds from the Cohesion Fund to allocate to the reception of refugees. We need, obviously, strong solidarity and we have managed, up to now, to show this strong solidarity. We have to find the right responses, the right resources, the right policies and the right investments in our economies, but also in our social systems. It has been said that it’s about two million today and there might be seven, eight or more million people who have to be received and welcomed, and who have to get all the support they need, especially in those Member States who are the ‘frontline’ Member States. I fully agree with you.

The European Union has allocated a budget specifically for the upcoming fiscal year. While this development represents positive progress, it underscores our readiness to address financial challenges by delivering a well-structured budget aligned with our strategic objectives. Our primary focus remains on fostering economic growth and job creation, leveraging investments in innovation, infrastructure, and educational programs such as the Erasmus scheme and COSME initiative. These measures aim to bolster the EU's competitive edge, ensuring sustained employment and economic vitality.

Additionally, we commit to supporting education and entrepreneurship through increased funding for both programs. Moreover, we reaffirm our commitment to safeguarding national interests by enhancing security institutions like Europol, Eurojust, and by addressing contemporary threats related to immigration and refugees. Despite the complexities encountered during negotiations, we successfully reached an agreement on the matter, emphasizing the necessity for future budgets to accommodate evolving needs and expectations from member states, thereby necessitating additional resources for implementation.

Mr President, we have a budget for the European Union for next year. This in itself is good news. Herewith we are proving to the Council, Commission and Parliament that we are ready to act and to deliver a budget to the European Union in these difficult circumstances and, not only that we have a budget, but that we have a good budget for the Union for next year. It responds to what we established at the beginning of the 7-year Multiannual Financial Framework as our main priorities: growth and jobs. In order to allow for more growth in the European economy to generate more jobs, we are investing the money which we have in innovation and infrastructure. It is those areas which will make the European economy stronger. We are also giving support to students, with EUR 50 million more for the Erasmus programme, and to SMEs and young entrepreneurs through investing EUR 50 million more into the COSME programme. As the Commissioner also said, we have not forgotten about farmers, and I want to see that, in addition to these additional priorities that we have had, we also respond to current and new challenges, making Europe safer again. We are strengthening Europol and Eurojust and we want to stop illegal migration to Europe and manage the refugee crisis which we have been tackling. The negotiations were difficult. We managed to agree, but to my colleagues in Council I also want to say that, if Europe is expected to deliver in the coming years, especially in 2018, Member States have to understand that, if there are new priorities and new demands vis—à-vis the budget of the Union, then of course fresh new resources are clearly also needed. What we are delivering this year is a good budget under difficult circumstances but, if we are expected to deliver more next year, then of course more resources are needed. To conclude, I would like to thank the main rapporteurs and the shadow rapporteurs, with whom we have worked in a good spirit. I would like to thank the Commissioner for the wisdom and the political sensitivity which she put into the negotiations – and sometimes passion too – to make this budget possible. I would also like to thank the Council for having been a good partner to us.

Madam-President, Mr. Andor: I gather from your remarks that the EC indeed pays attention to this issue. However, I believe its handling is somewhat restrained. As Mr. Ory previously stated during this debate, we do not lack knowledge regarding the activities performed by domestic workers within the EU; rather, we possess ample information that underscores the necessity for regulatory measures. In fact, approximately 5%-9%—a significant portion—are employed privately at home. These individuals constitute the lowest-paid segment of the workforce, lacking substantial legal protections and facing high rates of exploitation. Consequently, immediate action is required across various fronts, including the EC's involvement. Currently, the status quo cannot be salvaged through merely facilitating worker mobility. Therefore, initiatives such as those proposed under the I.L.O. Convention are commendable and warrant our backing. I propose addressing four critical areas:

1. Defining ambiguities in the current I.L.C. Convention. Should domestic workers not be permitted to perform tasks sporadistically or on an occasional basis, their ability to evade regulations is already compromised.

2. Implementing mandatory employment agreements throughout this sector. Such provisions are imperative to protect both employers and employees alike.

3. Establishing sound, universally applicable minimum wages while simultaneously ensuring fair employment standards.

4. Incorporating statutory social insurance obligations specifically targeting low-wage sectors like these domestic workers. Given their role as minimum-wage earners, they require comprehensive coverage under national social security systems.

Mrs. Berès' call for a standardized working environment is well-timed and aligns with my perspective. Moving forward, it is essential that any labor practice failing to meet basic living standards is eradicated from public discourse and societal acceptance.

Madam President, Mr Andor, ladies and gentlemen, I understand from what you have said, Mr Andor, that the European Commission is obviously taking notice of this subject. I also conclude that it is being handled with too much restraint. It is not the case, as Mr Őry emphasised previously in this discussion, that we know too little about what domestic workers in Europe do. On the contrary, we know so much that there is an urgent need for regulation. Between 5% and 9% of all employees work in private households. This is the worst paid group of employees in Europe, with the fewest rights and the largest proportion of illegal workers. Action is urgently needed in this area and this includes the European Commission. The situation we currently find ourselves in will not be improved by the free movement of workers. For this reason, the initiative in this International Labour Organisation (ILO) Convention is very welcome and deserves our support. I would like to comment on four points. Firstly, there are gaps in the definitions in the ILO Convention. If domestic workers cannot work occasionally, sporadically or not on an occupational basis in this sector, then the opportunities for circumventing the legislation are already in place. Secondly, there is an urgent need for mandatory employment contracts in this area. Thirdly, we must have not only sensible, generally binding minimum wage regulations, but also equal employment rights. Fourthly, these workers must be covered by a statutory social security obligation, in particular, given that this is a minimum wage sector. I am pleased that Mrs Berès has called in her statement for a campaign to introduce standard working conditions. This is a good thing. We must ensure in future that work which does not pay a living wage is outlawed by society and is no longer tolerated.

However, I believe its handling is somewhat restrained. Such provisions are imperative to protect both employers and employees alike. A significant portion of women experience physical or sex-based assaults during their lifetimes. This alarming statistic comes from a recent report by Ms. Maria Carlström, member of the ALDE group, emphasizing the urgent need for zero-tolerance measures against domestic violence across all European nations. Recognizing the growing prevalence of such abuses, the EU's Daphné II program allocates €50m annually over four years to protect vulnerable female populations. Despite these efforts, instances of child witnessing inhuman violence remain pervasive; estimates suggest around 5 to 10 million children witness or hear about such incidents annually. It underscores the importance of incorporating children into anti-violence strategies alongside adults, as their safety also demands attention. Thus, societal attitudes toward women's rights and violence against them must shift decisively to ensure comprehensive protection and justice for all affected individuals.

At least a third of women will at some point in their lives have been victims of a physical or sexual assault. That is the alarming figure taken from the excellent report by my colleague from the Group of the Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe, Mrs Maria Carlshamre. Faced with the escalation of violence against women and the way in which it is becoming an everyday phenomenon, there is only one firm, appropriate answer: zero tolerance whether the violence takes place in, or outside, the marriage. Europe has, moreover, faced up to its responsibilities in order to combat this form of violence affecting women from every social group. Under the Daphne II Programme, EUR 50 million are being dedicated between 2004-2008 to protecting those women among us who are most at risk. This is a symbolic amount because we are well aware that, if the prevention and victim support policies are to be effective, then they have to be carried out on a national basis. It is therefore alarming to learn that, each year, between five and ten million children see and hear inhuman acts of this kind take place. It is therefore crucial that the 25 national criminal sets of legislation from now on consider children, like their mothers, to be victims. Our society has for too long underestimated the seriousness of violent acts against women. We must put a stop to this cowardice.

The speaker agrees with previous remarks about the unacceptable state of affairs regarding women in伊朗， especially considering their role within the context of theEuropean Union. Despite efforts made during his tenure, the EU had to adhere to the nuclear deal struck under former US President Barack Obama, which he subsequently withdrew from without any prior consultation or agreement between both parties. This action is viewed as a form of double standard, as American policies towards Iran often prioritize male voices over those of female citizens.

Mr President, I just wanted to concur with what has been said by many speakers. The situation of women in Iran is unacceptable, and particularly from the point of view of the European Union, when I was a member of the Iranian delegation in the last mandate, we worked very hard to keep the nuclear accord in place, and the European Union stuck its neck out in that regard, but that must be reciprocated. As has been said, by particularly allowing women the freedom they deserve, and as one MEP said, men in Iran should not be telling women what to wear. I also concur with my colleague, Clare Daly, on the obligations of the United States in relation to Iran, and the nuclear accord. It was they who pulled out of it, and that was done despite the fact that there was an agreement done with the previous president, President Obama, so that in itself is also a cause for condemnation.

The US President, Mr. Trump, has repeatedly fantasized about committing war crimes against Iran. He lacks genuine concern for America; instead, he exhibits a superficial interest in self-preservation, akin to a deceitful, failed political figure portrayed in his imagination. Therefore, I appeal to fellow Europeans as representatives of global peace and justice to impose direct economic sanctions on this aggressive leader and those associated with him. These sanctions will effectively target the individual's personal wealth and financial resources, thereby serving as a potent deterrent. In particular, freezing his European bank accounts and restricting his travel post-retirement would significantly impact him. Furthermore, imposing trade embargoes on his favorite golf courses would serve as a tangible reminder of his unwarranted aggression.

However, we must also envision a brighter future. We should advocate for a return to responsible leadership from figures such as Bernie Sanders, who embodies the spirit of honesty and peace. His election as a beacon of hope could potentially alleviate the ongoing conflicts and secure a prosperous era for everyone.

Mr President, the President of the United States of America publicly and repeatedly fantasised about carrying out war crimes in Iran. The truth is President Trump doesn’t really care about America – like the shifty, failed, plastic mafia boss of his simple imagination, he only cares about himself. So my dear fellow Europeans, on behalf of world peace, in the name of justice, I call upon the European Union to impose sanctions directly on this warmonger and associates. The only deterrents this limited individual understands is what personally and financially hurts him. So freeze his European assets, restrict his travel once he is a private citizen again, place sanctions on his beloved golf courses, and let the lessons of Europe be clear: war criminals do not win. But, friends, let us talk of hope. The hope of peace. The hope of an honest president. America needs that hero to throw off the terror of an endless war, and to secure a brilliant future for all. That hero, that great hope, just could be Bernie Sanders.

Mr President,

I appreciate that this discussion is taking place here in Brussels. Concerning Amendment 3—on buildings—I wish to emphasize that Strasburg is a peaceful city, a hub of European cooperation. It was here that Robert Schumann laid the groundwork for the European Community. This context supports our future endeavors.

Additionally, the headquarters for ARTE, a broadcasting network dedicated to promoting European culture, underscores our commitment to preserving this legacy. We propose erecting another parliamentary studio adjacent to the ARTE premises, aiming to facilitate dialogue among Europeans on shaping their collective future.

Amendment 5 addresses a single member state. As agreed by all, we shall adhere to the treaties we've established. When discussing the creation of an EU History House in Amendment A4, it's imperative to highlight the importance of daily efforts toward maintaining peace and unity. Documenting these achievements ensures that future generations understand the significance of such actions.

Our annual visitor numbers at both the visitor center and Parliament itself exceed one million. The planned expansion into the new Parlementarium promises even greater engagement from nearly ten thousand attendees annually. These figures underscore the growing interest in understanding the historical evolution of Europe. Furthermore, we require robust European political entities to ensure solidarity against national conflicts and self-centered behaviors of individual states. An effective EU necessitates solid political organizations with adequate funding. Lastly, concerning Amendment B6 regarding MEPs' representation: ensuring visitors receive satisfactory service remains crucial. Therefore, tomorrow's vote carries particular weight.

Mr President, I am grateful that this debate is being held here in Strasbourg. With regard to Amendment 1 – buildings – I would like to say that Strasbourg is a city of peace, a place where bridges have been built in Europe. and it was here, for example, that one of our Presidents, Robert Schuman, was a founding father of the European Union. This also provides a basis for the future. This is the headquarters of Arte, a broadcaster that does a huge amount of work in bringing European culture to public attention. We should be proud of this and should consider the idea of building another studio for Parliament opposite the Arte studios, as a place for Europe to meet and set the agenda for the future. Amendment 2 refers to a single seat. That is what we have. Our single seat is Strasbourg. We should hold to the Treaties we have concluded. That goes without saying. When we come to the issue of a House of European History in Amendment 4, then it is our duty to show our younger generations how important it is to work for this peace every day. This needs to be documented thoroughly. We have over 1 million visitors per year to our visitor centre and here to Parliament itself; it is projected that the new Parliamentarium will have 100 000 visitors. People want to know how the history of Europe has developed. We also need strong European political parties for this. We need to show solidarity in the face of national conflict and the egoism of individual Member States. We need a strong European Union and this requires strong political parties. Naturally, these also need to be funded. In relation to Amendment 6 regarding representation: visitors do need to be looked after and have the right to expect proper, orderly services here in the House. That is why tomorrow’s vote is particularly important.

Madam Chairperson, distinguished delegates, this discussion underscores the shared commitment among us to the fate of the Syrians, their nation, and their broader region. Our aspiration unites us: a resolution to the ongoing conflict, durable peace for those affected, a viable pathway for returning displaced individuals, and long-term security for the entire region. This objective necessitates a genuine political framework as outlined by United Nations Security Council Resolutions 2192 and 2670. The current perilous situation serves as a poignant reminder of the potential dangers inherent in unresolved disputes, emphasizing the universal interest in aiding the aspirations expressed by the Syrian populace. I wish to reiterate once more that Syria remains a top priority for both the European Union and it will continue doing so. Furthermore, the EU stands resolute behind the sole means to achieve sustainable peace in Syria – a political settlement consistent with resolutions 2358 and 1640. We shall maintain our stance against any easing of sanctions or reconstruction efforts unless accompanied by substantial progress toward genuine political dialogue within the Syrian government.

Madam President, honourable Members, the debate today illustrates the importance we all attach to the Syrian conflict. We all want the same thing for the Syrian people, for Syria, for the region: an end to the conflict; a lasting peace for the Syrian people; the possibility of a voluntary, safe and dignified return of refugees; and stability for the region as a whole. This goal can only be achieved through a real political process, as set out by UN Security Council Resolution 2254. The dire regional situation is a stark reminder that unsolved conflict can easily degenerate, and that we all have a strong interest in supporting the aspirations of the Syrian people. Let me also emphasise again that Syria and the Syrian people remain a priority for the EU and will continue to do so, and the EU will continue to raise its voice for the only path to sustainable peace for Syria: a political solution in line with UN Security Council Resolution 2254. We will remain firm in our position on Syria. There will be no lifting of sanctions and no reconstruction in the absence of a serious and credible engagement from the Syrian regime in the political process.

We shall maintain our stance against any easing of sanctions or reconstruction efforts unless accompanied by substantial progress toward genuine political dialogue within the Syrian government. Madam Chairperson, distinguished delegates, this discussion underscores the shared commitment among us to the fate of the Syrians, their nation, and their broader region. This objective necessitates a genuine political framework as outlined by United Nations Security Council Resolutions 2192 and 2670. I wish to reiterate once more that Syria remains a top priority for both the European Union and it will continue doing so. Furthermore, the EU stands resolute behind the sole means to achieve sustainable peace in Syria – a political settlement consistent with resolutions 2358 and 1640. Our aspiration unites us: a resolution to the ongoing conflict, durable peace for those affected, a viable pathway for returning displaced individuals, and long-term security for the entire region. The current perilous situation serves as a poignant reminder of the potential dangers inherent in unresolved disputes, emphasizing the universal interest in aiding the aspirations expressed by the Syrian populace.

Madam President, honourable Members, the debate today illustrates the importance we all attach to the Syrian conflict. We all want the same thing for the Syrian people, for Syria, for the region: an end to the conflict; a lasting peace for the Syrian people; the possibility of a voluntary, safe and dignified return of refugees; and stability for the region as a whole. We will remain firm in our position on Syria. Let me also emphasise again that Syria and the Syrian people remain a priority for the EU and will continue to do so, and the EU will continue to raise its voice for the only path to sustainable peace for Syria: a political solution in line with UN Security Council Resolution 2254. This goal can only be achieved through a real political process, as set out by UN Security Council Resolution 2254. There will be no lifting of sanctions and no reconstruction in the absence of a serious and credible engagement from the Syrian regime in the political process. The dire regional situation is a stark reminder that unsolved conflict can easily degenerate, and that we all have a strong interest in supporting the aspirations of the Syrian people.

The real objective of these documents was elucidated during Ms. Costello's statement. This situation constitutes a deliberate maneuver orchestrated by the Trio within this parliament - comprising the Epp, the SDP, and the Lib Dems - to evade accountability. Her remarks can be succinctly summarized as "the Trio directed us to undertake this action." Is it truly sufficient for the Labour and other parties present here to offer such an explanation? Could the dog have consumed every single election manifesto? Or did the Labour party bear any responsibility for cutting into healthcare, education, and social benefits? For instance, were they involved in reducing taxes on households or levying property taxes, water taxes? From an Irish perspective, these documents pertain to Fianna Fail, Green Party, Fine Ireland, and Labour achieving a semblance of clean hands while declaring, "bail-ins for senior bond holders weren't an option open to the state." However, this claim stands false; it represents a political decision made jointly by the trio and also by European establishments aiming to exploit Irish workers rather than targeting bondholders or speculative investors. These documents adhere to the same rationale espoused by Bono; he argues that Irish citizens saved Irish banks. Yet, this narrative overlooks the fact that Irish individuals were subjected to bullying, threats, and coercion under the guise of supporting a rescue package intended primarily for wealthy bankers and investors. In response to the humanitarian crisis precipitated by the Trios' policies, what is required beyond merely critiquing the legality of their actions? A rejection of autocratic neoliberal ideology is necessary. Furthermore, it demands refusal to pay debts that are not ours. Investment in the public sector should serve to create employment opportunities, and building a Europe dedicated to the masses over millionaires is essential.

Madam President, the real purpose of these reports was made clear in Emer Costello’s speech earlier. What we have here is an act of misdirection by the Troika inside this Parliament – the EPP, the Social Democrats and the Liberals – to absolve themselves of all responsibility. Her speech could be summed up with the phrase: the Troika told us to do it. Is that really the best excuse that the Labour Party and the others here can come up with? Did the dog also eat your election manifestos? Did the Labour Party have nothing to do with the cuts to health, education and social welfare? Did they have nothing to do with the household tax, the property tax, the water tax? In an Irish context these reports means Fianna Fáil, the Greens, Fine Gael and Labour being able to wash their hands and say – and I quote from the report – ‘the bail-in of senior bondholders was not an option available to the Irish authorities’. The only problem is, it is just not true. It was a political choice, made by the Troika, yes, but also made by the establishment parties across Europe, to burn Irish working-class people instead of hitting the bondholders and speculators. These reports accept the very same logic. It is the same logic of Bono, patronisingly telling us that the Irish people bailed out the Irish people. No, Mr Hewson. On Planet Earth the Irish people, with the treacherous connivance of two governments, were bullied, threatened and coerced in order to bail out billionaire bankers and bondholders by the very people he described as heroes. Confronted by the humanitarian catastrophe created by the Troika policies, we need more than criticisms of the legal basis of the Troika. We need a rejection of authoritarian neo-liberalism. We need to refuse to pay the debt that is not our debt. We need public investment to create jobs and we need to build a Europe for the millions, not the millionaires.

In light of your previous speech, I shall revert to the status quo. The recent multilateral agreement concerning the marine stock management framework in Western Waters and adjacent areas holds substantial importance. This not only reflects the difficulty encountered in reaching similar agreements in the historical past but also signifies a robust commitment aimed at ensuring the sustainable utilization of fish populations across various jurisdictions including Belgium, Germany, France, Ireland, Spain, Portugal, and, of course, the United Kingdom. Recognizing the considerable hurdles facing the fishing industry in these regions, particularly in my electorate along the southern coastline of Ireland, where there are significant challenges related to the implementation of fisheries policies focused on particular species. These challenges manifest as complex issues stemming from mixed catch compositions, coupled with the requirement to land certain catches and the impossibility of discarding them, thereby complicating operations. Consequently, I am gratified to observe this accord incorporating all pertinent stocks under a unified management strategy, thereby eliminating the need for separate single-species management frameworks, thereby offering greater operational flexibility. Sustainable stock levels are imperative; thus, their inclusion remains paramount. Furthermore, I commend the 'my friend' Mr Cadex and the commissioner, Mr Valeta, for securing this resolution regarding recreational fisheries, emphasizing that any limitations imposed on recreational activities must be grounded in scientific evidence supported by rigorous objective criteria, which is a positive development within the accord.

Mr President, after the last speech I will come back to normality. The agreement that was recently reached on the multiannual plan for fish stocks in the western waters and adjacent waters is an important one. Not only because it has proven difficult to find agreement on such plans in the past, but because it is a strong agreement that will help to ensure the sustainable exploitation of stocks in Belgian, German, French, Irish, Spanish, Portuguese and, yes, United Kingdom waters. We are all aware of the challenges faced by the fishing sector in the western waters. Fishers in my constituency in the southern coast of Ireland face significant challenges due to the obligations of our fisheries policies targeting specific species in this region. It is difficult due to catches being mixed and this, combined with the landing obligation and inability to discard catches, makes life difficult. I am pleased, therefore, to see this agreement incorporate all relevant stocks into a single management plan, replacing the existing single species which would allow for some flexibility for fisheries. Sustainable stocks are vital and that’s what we have to include. Finally, I welcome the agreement on recreational fisheries. Any restrictions on recreational fishing should be based on scientific evidence and objective criteria. This is a welcome inclusion in the agreement. I congratulate ‘mon ami’ Mr Cadec and the Commissioner, Mr Vella.

Madam, Bangladesh ranks second globally among apparel exporters, contributing approximately 83% to the nation's total export earnings. The majority of the workforce in the Ready-Made Garment sector comprises young women hailing from rural areas. In October 2011, the Rana Tower collapse claimed 1,101 lives and left over 2,504 injured. These incidents did not mark the end of tragedies; others occurred subsequently. The incident at Rana Palace highlighted the necessity for a safe work culture within the garment industry both domestically and internationally.

Over the past decade, significant improvements have been observed, yet much remains to be accomplished. Implementing clear labor standards, adhering to guidelines, and establishing robust accident reporting systems at various levels are essential. Ensuring compliance with fundamental labor standards and enforcing national legislation concerning compensation, employment terms, gender inclusion, and working hours is paramount.

Enhanced accessibility to social protections for workers throughout Bangladesh across all economic segments is indispensable. Strengthening the capacities of safety committees (SHCs), labor inspection offices, and trade union organizations is vital. Safeguarding labor rights ensures freedom for trade unions, prevents harassment, and facilitates better governmental-employer-trade union dialogue. It underscores the importance of building on the lessons and achievements in the garment sector to promote occupational safety and improve conditions in other critical industries.

Madam President, Bangladesh is the second largest apparel exporter in the world. The sector accounts for about 82% of the country’s total export revenue and most workers in the ready—made garment sector are young women, as you said, from rural backgrounds. Ten years ago, the deadliest accident in the history of the modern garment industry –and one of the worst industrial accidents ever – took place in Rana Plaza in Bangladesh, leaving 1 100 people dead and at least 2 500 grievously injured. This was not the first tragedy of its kind and, sadly, not the last. Others have happened since. Rana Plaza and the other industrial accidents suffered by Bangladesh brought home the urgent need to establish a culture of workplace safety in the country’s garment industry and beyond. Much has improved in the last ten years, however more needs to be done to ensure that clear standards, guidelines and accident reporting protocols are understood and implemented at all levels. Compliance with core labour standards and national legislation must be improved, including coverage of compensation, contracts, gender inclusivity and working time. Better access to social protection for workers across Bangladesh in all economic sectors is crucial. Further strengthening of the capacity of the safety committees, labour inspectorate and trade unions will be integral to this. Labour rights must be respected in order to foster an environment where trade unions can operate freely and without harassment, where improved social dialogue between the government, employers and trade unions takes place. It is imperative that we build upon the lessons learned and progress made in the ready—made garment industry to enhance industrial safety in other priority sectors to avoid such a tragedy repeating itself in the future. Occupational safety and health must be a priority.

The Parliament stands firm against the disregard of its democratic responsibilities regarding the Union's programs. While supporting the participation and cooperation of New Zealan and other member states, the institution asserts its right to oversee and approve all such collaborations through its legislative body. This stance has been reiterated over nearly two years, leading to diplomatic efforts aimed at ensuring meaningful consent from the Parliament. Should the Council fail to comply with these demands, the resolution calls for outright denial of consent, thereby nullifying any proposed associations.

Mr President, Madam Commissioner, colleagues, there are two things we need to make clear, very clear today. First, Parliament is a strong supporter of the participation of New Zealand in the Union’s programmes. We are strong supporters of other countries like the Faroe Islands, Canada and South Korea participating in our programmes. The resolution we will adopt today makes it very clear we have no problem with those countries and their participation in the Union’s programmes. However, and this is the second point, Parliament will not accept that its democratic prerogatives are disregarded. The Treaties are clear: only Parliament’s consent can make the Union plausibly accept to set up a committee under the international agreement with the power to amend certain parts of the international agreement. I repeat: only if Parliament gives consent. The Court of Justice also made clear that Parliament’s consent must be meaningful. The Council and the Commission have chosen to ignore this constitutional reality. They have insisted on setting up association agreements for the Union programmes with a completely new category of third countries, which cut the parliaments out completely. They have asked the Parliament to sign a chèque en blanc. Therefore, the Parliament cannot give meaningful consent to these agreements, and we have communicated this clearly to other institutions for almost two years. It’s an insult of the services, and I addressed specifically the Secretary-General that she is refusing to take up interinstitutional dialogue, although Parliament’s President had asked for that a year ago. So this resolution is our last attempt to change the course of the Council and the Commission. The only step left us after this is to start denying consent, which will kill these agreements. We do not want to do this: researchers involved in Horizon should not become the victim of this interinstitutional fight, but we protect our democratic prerogatives. We will do that, whatever it takes. So I would have to read you the following question, also in the name of my co-rapporteur, Mr Lacapelle. As regards international agreements dedicated to the participation of third countries in the Union programmes, the Commission is asked the following: given the broad powers allocated to the Joint Committee established under the draft agreement between the EU and New Zealand – including its ability to adopt and amend the protocols on associations to Union programmes without further parliamentary involvement – how does the Commission intend to ensure that Parliament retains oversight and power of consent as regards future associations to the Union programmes? Secondly, can the Commission commit to increase transparency of the implementation of the agreements via regular and detailed reporting in order to enable Parliament to exercise its oversight rights? Will the Commission structure future agreements in a way that guarantees the respect of Parliament’s prerogatives concerning agreements on any association to a Union programme? Will the Commission launch negotiations on an interinstitutional agreement that completely abides by both the letter and the spirit of the Treaties in full respect of the principles of parliamentary scrutiny in the implementation of the association agreements, including as regards the adoption and amendment of protocols? And, I may add, this might be today a conversation of connoisseurs, and we might not have a lot of participation because it’s very specific. Believe me, there will be a lot of presence if Parliament is going to deny consent to these agreements.

Honourable members of parliament, Mr president, I wish to express my gratitude for this occasion where I have the privilege of discussing this critical matter. It has become particularly relevant given the unanimous decision made by the United Nations General Assembly in June regarding the convening of a high-profile meeting dedicated to addressing HIV/AIDS. In light of the ongoing challenge posed by the global coronavirus pandemic, which necessitates urgent attention, the situation of the AIDS pandemic remains distressingly dire, especially considering the severe repercussions on health services such as HIV, Hepatitis, Tuberculosis, and Malaria.

We understand that HIV thrives in environments marked by disparities, exacerbates due to conflict zones or natural disasters, and disproportionately affects marginalized groups. Therefore, concerted efforts are imperative to address these inequalities effectively, preserving the progress made over the past ten years and securing universal access for those affected by key populations.

This recognition led the Commission to endorse the newly developed Global AIDS Strategy for the years 21st to 26th, emphasizing the prioritization of tackling inequalities and promoting gender equality along with human rights.

By focusing on individuals and communities, integrating them into policy-making processes, and learning from the lessons drawn from the COVID-19 pandemic, it becomes evident that building sustainable healthcare systems is foundational. This entails fostering comprehensive health care infrastructure alongside providing adequate funding sources, especially through national budgets, while ensuring equitable accessibility to essential services and medicines.

In addition, the fight to eradicate HIV should prioritize the needs of adolescents and young adults. Our renewed Gender Action Programme underscores this commitment to combating gender inequality across our strategies. Furthermore, the European Commission's longstanding dedication towards eradicating HIV/AIDS is supported by significant contributions through the Global Fund, totalling €55 million over two years, highlighting their substantial investment in this area.

Our involvement in negotiating a forward-looking political declaration aims to tackle the complex nature of the challenges faced by the AIDS crisis. These include new prevention goals, enhanced testing and therapy targets, and specific initiatives aimed at reducing stigma, discriminating behavior, and protecting human rights among key populations. We believe that this initiative is directly tied to the dismantling of barriers, thereby contributing to the broader goal of eliminating HIV infection.

The upcoming High-Level Meeting offers a pivotal moment to reinvigorate global cooperation against the HIV/AIDS epidemic, and we remain committed to participating fully.

Mr President, honourable Members of Parliament, thank you for this opportunity to discuss this important issue. The debate is very timely, as was said by the author himself, in view of the decision of the UN General Assembly to convene a high-level meeting on HIV and AIDS in June. As the world tackles the COVID pandemic, the devastating AIDS global pandemic continues. While the world focuses on overcoming the direct impact of COVID and laying the ground for recovery, the secondary health impacts, the disruptions to essential health services, including with respect to HIV, hepatitis, tuberculosis and malaria, are deeply concerning. We all know that HIV is fuelled by inequalities and that it disproportionately affects vulnerable and marginalised populations. Worsening inequalities increase the spread of HIV as do armed conflicts and natural disasters. We need to work collectively to ensure that our response to the COVID pandemic tackles these inequalities, preserve the gains achieved during the last decade and secures access for key populations to the essential services. This is why the Commission welcomes the new global AIDS strategy for 2021-2026, sharing many of its priorities, not least the focus on tackling inequalities, gender equality and human rights. Putting people at the centre, involving communities and ensuring that we learn from COVID, in particular to build sustainable health systems is fundamental. This is why we welcome the broad principles, reiterated commitments and strong calls contained in the resolution tabled today. The European Union is actively involved in the negotiation of an ambitious and progressive new political declaration for the upcoming high-level meeting on HIV-AIDS. As with previous United Nations political declarations, the most sensitive issues will require delicate negotiations. On behalf of the European Union, the Commission is recommending the inclusion, not only of new prevention, testing and treatment targets, but also of specific targets related to stigma, discrimination and human rights. I am convinced that the fight against HIV is closely linked to breaking down barriers, including working on legal issues ensuring equitable access of key populations to services and treatments and strengthening the overall health systems, accompanied by an enabling environment for sustainable financial resources, notably domestic. The response to the HIV pandemic also requires further focus on adolescent girls and young women. Our new Gender Action Plan 3, puts the fight against gender inequality at the centre of our action. The Commission is a long-standing supporter of the fight against HIV. We are a major contributor to the global fund with EUR 550 million for the period 2020-2022 and we attach high importance to fighting HIV-AIDS globally. Our more recent support to the work of the act accelerator, notably its cross-cutting health system connector, is paramount to mitigating the indirect effects of the COVID pandemic and to strengthening the preparedness and response to current and future pandemics, and this includes HIV-AIDS. The upcoming high-level meeting provides an important opportunity to remobilise the international community with respect to the fight against HIV-AIDS, and you can count on the EU’s engagement.

Our more recent support to the work of the act accelerator, notably its cross-cutting health system connector, is paramount to mitigating the indirect effects of the COVID pandemic and to strengthening the preparedness and response to current and future pandemics, and this includes HIV-AIDS. The debate is very timely, as was said by the author himself, in view of the decision of the UN General Assembly to convene a high-level meeting on HIV and AIDS in June. We need to work collectively to ensure that our response to the COVID pandemic tackles these inequalities, preserve the gains achieved during the last decade and secures access for key populations to the essential services. The European Union is actively involved in the negotiation of an ambitious and progressive new political declaration for the upcoming high-level meeting on HIV-AIDS. As the world tackles the COVID pandemic, the devastating AIDS global pandemic continues. Putting people at the centre, involving communities and ensuring that we learn from COVID, in particular to build sustainable health systems is fundamental. While the world focuses on overcoming the direct impact of COVID and laying the ground for recovery, the secondary health impacts, the disruptions to essential health services, including with respect to HIV, hepatitis, tuberculosis and malaria, are deeply concerning. We are a major contributor to the global fund with EUR 550 million for the period 2020-2022 and we attach high importance to fighting HIV-AIDS globally. Mr President, honourable Members of Parliament, thank you for this opportunity to discuss this important issue. This is why we welcome the broad principles, reiterated commitments and strong calls contained in the resolution tabled today. As with previous United Nations political declarations, the most sensitive issues will require delicate negotiations. On behalf of the European Union, the Commission is recommending the inclusion, not only of new prevention, testing and treatment targets, but also of specific targets related to stigma, discrimination and human rights. Worsening inequalities increase the spread of HIV as do armed conflicts and natural disasters. I am convinced that the fight against HIV is closely linked to breaking down barriers, including working on legal issues ensuring equitable access of key populations to services and treatments and strengthening the overall health systems, accompanied by an enabling environment for sustainable financial resources, notably domestic. This is why the Commission welcomes the new global AIDS strategy for 2021-2026, sharing many of its priorities, not least the focus on tackling inequalities, gender equality and human rights. The response to the HIV pandemic also requires further focus on adolescent girls and young women.

Madam Chairperson, esteemed colleagues, it is abundantly evident that combating corruption remains a paramount concern within this institution. I extend my heartfelt gratitude to you for demonstrating profound insight, unwavering dedication, and unequivocal principles. Corruption jeopardizes economic prosperity, societal harmony, and adherence to legal frameworks; therefore, concerted efforts towards its eradication are imperative. Recognizing the urgent need to address these issues given the allocation of fiscal resources for reconstruction and reform, I acknowledge the inquiries posed by individuals such as Mr. López Aguilera, Ms. Yonchova, Ms., and others seeking additional measures from the Commission. Although the Commission lacks authority to conduct criminal investigations independently, the Eurojust organization and the Europol entity possess the requisite capabilities. In November of the previous year, the Europassive Police Office recommended the recuperation of €6 million attributable to misconduct in the Interior Ministry of Bulgaria. However, the commission may further expand its influence through initiatives like the annual Anti-Corruption Report integrated into the Rule of the Land Mechanism, with particular emphasis on incorporating recommendations. Furthermore, I express solidarity with Commissioner Freund regarding the readiness of the Commission's stance toward supporting Member States, including Bulgaria, in developing and implementing anticorruption mechanisms alongside appropriate safeguard provisions for public prosecution. Additionally, the proposal to establish a new specialized anti-money-lauzing agency has been approved. Lastly, I commend the ongoing investigation of corruption linked to Union funds, emphasizing the significance of the newly operational Eurojust office. I remain committed to ensuring that our collective resolve continues to confront corrupt practices until they are eliminated.

Madam President, honourable Members, this debate made clear that the fight against corruption is a priority of this House. I would like to warmly thank you for your deep knowledge, your commitment and your clear values. Corruption undermines our economy, society and the rule of law. We must fight corruption, especially now when we are mobilising public money for recovery and reforms. I hear Mr López Aguilar, Ms Yoncheva, Ms in ‘t Veld and many of you asking me and the Commission to do more. I hear you and I agree. The Commission does not have the competence nor the capacity to carry out criminal investigations, but the European Public Prosecutor’s Office (EPPO) and the European Anti-Fraud Office (OLAF) have. In December last year, OLAF recommended the recovery of EUR 6 million due to abuse of power in the Bulgarian Ministry of Interior. But the Commission can also do more. We now have again an annual Anti—Corruption Report, as part of the annual Rule of Law Report, and one important step will be to make this Rule of Law Mechanism sharper by introducing recommendations. I will also say to Mr Freund that of course the Commission stands ready to support Bulgaria or any Member States to design and implement mechanisms to fight corruption and money-laundering, and to have proper safeguards for public prosecutions. In July, the Commission proposed the setting up of a new anti-money laundering supervisor agency, and we will, of course, continue to prioritise the actions under the EU strategy to tackle organised crime. Corruption in relation to the Union budget is also to be investigated. As I said earlier, the European Public Prosecutor’s Office is now in place. I would like to thank you again for this debate. I must say that, as long as corruption undermines the rule of law, we have not done enough. I’m glad to see once more that this House shows how strong an ally you are in the fight against corruption.

Additionally, the proposal to establish a new specialized anti-money-lauzing agency has been approved. Madam Chairperson, six months ago, the voice once again belonged to Daphna Caruano Gálzio. An incorruptible and courageous voice committed to combating corruption and defending human rights - our rights - and her murder is a devastating blow to everyone. This attack hits close to home; it threatens our very fabric. Thus, we must demand accountability from those responsible. We must seek justice not just for Dafna's family, but for ourselves as well. The killers, along with their accomplices - the middle men, the mastersmokers, those who provide political cover - must be brought to book. There should be no impunity in Malta.

However, if we hope to achieve justice, we need laws that protect citizens. If we cannot rely on impartial investigations while the Prime Minster remains entrenched in power, then we will never receive the closure we deserve. Are we truly naïve enough to believe that those who have obstructed justice for two years would now take the lead in demanding justice? Is it possible they could actually rise above their own actions and genuinely pursue the vindication Dafni deserves?

The Venice Commission warned nearly a year before about the risks posed by Prime Minister Musat's expansive powers. These dangers are manifest in Malta today, hour by hour, day by day. Each moment he remains in power is another chance for justice to slip away from us. So, my dear colleagues:

Let me conclude with the single Maltesian word I understand. "Prime Minister, leave!"

Madam President, 26 months ago the voice of Daphne Caruana Galizia was silenced: a brave and fearless voice dedicated to fighting corruption and to standing up for human rights – for our rights – and her assassination is a blow to us all. It strikes at the heart of our society. And therefore we must fight for justice for Daphne. The murderers, but also everybody else involved – the middlemen, the masterminds, those providing political cover – must be identified and prosecuted. Impunity must end in Malta. But how can we expect justice when there is no rule of law? How can we trust in an independent investigation as long as the Prime Minister continues to cling to power? Can we really expect the same people that have been obstructing justice for the past 26 months, the same people that have been demolishing the memorials that were erected in honour of Daphne’s memory – can we really expect these people now suddenly to lead the charge in seeking justice that the family of Daphne so deserves? How naive are we exactly supposed to be? The Venice Commission already concluded over a year ago that the wide powers of the Prime Minister create a serious risk for the rule of law, and we see that risk now in Malta day after day after day. And every minute longer this Prime Minister stays in the centre of power is a minute where justice can escape for us. Therefore, dear colleagues, let me finish with the only word in Maltese that I know. ‘Prime Minister Muscat: barra!’

Madam Chairperson, our House frequently debates freedom of press across Europe, including Italy's Prime Minister Berlusconic issues. Should Western nations criticize Italy regarding press freedoms, they risk appearing domineering towards a sovereign nation. Following an OSCE representative’s report on press freedom in July, several cases surfaced internationally:

- Greece saw the death of a Blogger named Socrates.
- Latvian journalist Grigorian Nemcovic died under suspicious circumstances.
- Hungary enacted a restrictive media law undermining public TV independence.
- French President Sarkozzi monitored public broadcasting.
- Romania faced a controversial media regulation.

Additionally, recent Italian legislation bans investigative reporters from using wiretapped material.

Turkey, Serbia and Albania, aspiring EU members, face OSCE rebukes for media regulations. Despite their potential EU membership, the OSSE continues oversight due to member states’ failure to apply similar scrutiny internally.

The OSCE acts as a stern deterrent against media consolidation. However, some argue Italy retains its EU voting status while continuing to suppress journalism and remain dominated by a single business magnate and Prime Minister. This raises concerns over the validity of the OSCEE benchmarks applied universally.

Ultimately, the European Parliament seeks action against media monopolies. It calls upon the Commission to propose measures to address media concentration effectively, acknowledging the OSSEE's seriousness in scrutinizing media freedom.

Madam President, this House regularly discusses freedom of the press both within and outside Europe and, like you, I remember a heated debate in the autumn of 2009 on freedom of the press in Italy, among other countries. Was it legitimate for us Europeans to point the finger at Italy and its prime minister Mr Berlusconi, or was it a domestic matter in which the rest of Europe should not have been interfering? In July, the Representative on Freedom of the Media of the Organisation for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) published her report. She did dare to point the finger. The OSCE has done the rounds in Europe and drawn our attention to the following countries. The murder of a blogger, Socrates Giolias, in Greece, the murder of a journalist, Grigorijs Nemcovs, in Latvia, the new media law in Hungary that encroaches on the autonomy of public TV channels and decreases the available media, and the presidential supervision of public channels in France by President Sarkozy. I have just heard something about Romania, and then, of course, there are the latest developments in Italy whereby a law prohibits investigative journalists from reproducing wire tapped materials. Turkey, Serbia, Albania and Bosnia-Herzegovina, too – countries with the prospect of joining the European Union – receive reprimands from the OSCE, and rightly so. A big stick remains for these countries, however: membership of the European Union. Freedom of the press is one of our Copenhagen criteria. Therefore, benchmarking is used for accession countries, and it is distressing to see that the European Council does not consider these benchmarks applicable to itself. If it were up to the Group of the Greens/European Free Alliance, Italy would indeed lose its voting rights in the Council whilst it persisted in the repression of journalists and whilst everything continued to be in the hands of a single businessman and prime minister. To return to the subject of media concentration, however, the fact that commercial and public media in Italy are in the same hands is well known, but let us not forget that there are also Axel Springer, Bertelsmann, Rupert Murdoch and, in the Netherlands and Belgium, Christian van Thillo, who owns a very large number of newspapers. A free press is crucial to our democracy, and it is up to the European Commission to present us with a proposal to combat media concentration, precisely because we have to take the OSCE seriously.

Additionally, recent Italian legislation bans investigative reporters from using wiretapped material. - Greece saw the death of a Blogger named Socrates. Ultimately, the European Parliament seeks action against media monopolies. Firstly, this discussion has consumed considerable time during our tenure. Notably, the initiative undertaken by Parliament has led to an evident change in both the Council's stance and that of the commission. Commissioner Semeta seems genuinely committed to this reform, and his enthusiasm is commendable.

However, the core challenge at hand revolves around ensuring that this tax measure is applied broadly across a wide taxpayer base and levied uniformly. This involves determining the extent to which we can incorporate all potential taxpayers while mitigating the risk of competitive disadvantages arising from disparities in tax treatment among different jurisdictions. Therefore, the focus now lies on refining our approach to ensure compliance with these criteria.

We are considering amendments to the initial proposal, incorporating elements of issuance, ownership, and currency transactions—areas where there appears to be consensus among stakeholders. For instance, the inclusion of currency transactions aligns well with the starting premise of the Tobin Tax debate. Furthermore, the exclusion of non-executed trades introduces a regulatory framework aimed at curbing high-frequency financial activities, thereby enhancing transparency and reducing arbitrage opportunities.

In addition, the committee's recommendation to exempt certain sectors like UCITS from taxes may undermine investor interests, particularly in light of the OECD's findings regarding the profitability of pension fund investments under current regulations. Similarly, the proposal to exempt pension funds entirely from taxation might inadvertently benefit banks by facilitating tax-free operations within the sector. These exemptions would serve neither the public interest nor the economic health of these institutions.

Lastly, addressing the Council, Denmark, a nation known for its stringent taxation policies, stands to gain significantly from advancements in capital yield taxation. Despite your achievements thus far, there remains much room for improvement in this critical domain. Consequently, I urge you to expedite your deliberations and adopt Parliament's proposals rather than focusing solely on alternative strategies.

Mr President, Commissioner Šemeta, ladies and gentlemen, firstly, this debate has already taken up quite a lot of our time in this period of office. The interesting development is that precisely through Parliament’s efforts, we have achieved a clear shift in the Council’s position and in the position of the Commission. Commissioner Šemeta, I am pleased that you have truly had a Damascene conversion on this. That is pleasing, and I fully endorse what you have said here. However, the background that we must discuss is how we can implement this tax as generally and widely as possible on a broad tax base and as evenly as possible. That is precisely what we are wrestling with now. In other words, the issue is whether we will succeed in including as many taxpayers as possible, based on the proposed residence principle, so as to avoid competitive disadvantage. Parliament is therefore proposing that we supplement the Commission’s proposal by adding the principles of issuance and ownership. I would be pleased if you could examine this very carefully. In my opinion, and in that of many experts, this makes the tax stronger; it ensures that it can be levied more evenly. Of course, we immediately start now to debate its scope of application. Firstly, currency transactions. We propose that currency transactions, which is where the whole debate on a Tobin tax began, be included. We do not find the legal concerns that have been asserted in that area to be very convincing, nor do various experts. We think it is right to include these currency transactions. My second point concerns the issue of high-frequency trading. In this case, we are proposing that transactions that are not completed are also included in the tax base, since in practice, the number of instructions that are not executed, not completed, is 15 times as high as the number completed. The tax would then also put a brake on high-frequency trading. I regret to say that the Group of the European People’s Party (Christian Democrats) is currently proposing a general exemption in this area for the undertakings for collective investment in transferable securities (UCITS). Exempting the large investment funds market would be a serious mistake. It would mean that banks would simply establish a UCITS and thereby be able to carry out their transactions without any tax liability. That would be doing ourselves a real disservice. On the matter of pension funds, I regret to say that the Committee on Economic and Monetary Affairs is now also proposing that pension funds be exempted. That, too, is a mistake in our view, because studies show very clearly that the less frequently that pension funds trade, the more profitable they are for their investors in the end. The OECD has just presented such a study. Implementing this tax consistently will not penalise them. Finally, I would also like to address the Council. The Danish Presidency – a country with a high tax burden, including on the factor of labour – should have a particularly great interest in progress being made in the area of the taxation of capital yields. So far, you have achieved little in this respect. Please would you get moving on this, and instead of looking into alternatives, just go with Parliament’s proposals.

I am pleased to endorse this resolution. As members of the Labor group, along with Socialists and Democrats, we have consistently advocated for these enhanced regulations designed to elevate worker standards and confer substantial benefits upon numerous individuals employed under precarious zero-hour contracts or operating within the gig sector. Despite efforts by some to minimize the impact of such employment arrangements, my constituents continue to grapple with uncertainty regarding their financial security. However, progress toward addressing this issue has commenced. We now seek an overhaul of labor practices, ensuring fair wages and protections for the majority rather than just the privileged few. Europe's evolution reflects its commitment to enhancing workplace conditions for all, not just select segments of society.

Mr President, I am delighted to support this report. Labour MEPs, alongside our Socialist and Democrat colleagues, have fought tooth and nail for these new rules that will improve conditions and give new rights to millions of workers on insecure zero—hours contracts and working in the gig economy. I am sick and tired of hearing from people who work hard, but don’t know from one week to the next, whether they will be able to afford to pay the bills. But the fight back has begun. An end to work cancelled at the last minute without compensation. Workers given enough to notice about new shifts. No more paying for your own training. Europe is changing because we are voting to make it better. A fair deal at work for the many, not the few.
