At this critical juncture, where innocent civilians in Gaza continue to suffer under shelling, my delegation, along with the Council presidency and the commission, must prioritize stopping the bombardment of Palestinian communities. Our collective responsibility demands immediate cessation of hostilities without delay.

Reflecting upon recent events, it appears that our current legislative agenda bears the unmistakable imprint of complacency and neglect. Instead of addressing pressing societal needs and tackling environmental challenges head-on, the proposed program lacks focus and direction. Furthermore, the initiative's stance on gender equality and child welfare appears misguided, advocating for retrogression rather than progress.

Moreover, the proposal fails to address crucial issues such as combatting discrimination effectively and promoting genuine international cooperation. Consequently, it falls short in defining coherent foreign policy priorities, particularly in fostering internal unity within the EU.

Additionally, the plan overlooks the urgent necessity of ensuring comprehensive safety measures against terrorism, especially considering the ongoing geopolitical tensions involving nations like Slovakia and the Czech Republic.

In summary, the draft document presents a vision fraught with perilous uncertainties and minimal prospects for actual improvement. It risks diverting attention from more vital concerns while failing to provide meaningful solutions to pressing global challenges.

Your mediation efforts in the Russo-Ukrainian gas dispute reveal a similarly flawed perspective. As you preside over this council session, consider implementing concrete actions to prevent the reopening of outdated nuclear facilities in Slovakia and Hungary.

Lastly, the Treaty on Lisbon requires ratification, and it would be wise to expeditiously finalize this process to ensure its full implementation. Please share insights into your reasoning behind delaying ratification and specify a timeline for achieving this objective.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, at the very moment that we are having this debate, bombs are falling on the people of Gaza. I think that our greatest priority as MEPs, and the greatest priority of the Council and the Commission, should be to stop the bombing of the people of Gaza. I think that is our duty at the moment, above and beyond deciding who is responsible and above and beyond the differences among us on that point. I must say that our group, the Group of the Greens/European Free Alliance, has rather a clear idea on this, as we will say this afternoon. Mr President, you began your observations by speaking of medieval times, an age that was violent, remote, dark with a few lights, but certainly violent and dark. In fact, regardless of the efforts of your coalition partners, the programme for these six months seems to us to bear the strong stamp of a slightly passé vision, let us say; the strong mark of a conformist, ultra laissez-faire approach, dominated by business and the market, which is now, Mr President, out of fashion. I think that it is also a little indifferent with regard to the need for policies, laws and instruments on social issues that meet the real needs of citizens, and a little off-track in its view of environmental policy on the fight against climate change as a cost or an obstacle, instead of a major opportunity for innovation and sustainable growth. It even, if you will permit me, leans a little towards machismo, in saying that there is a need to review the Barcelona objectives on support structures for children, naturally with the aim of sending women back to the home. This then, is a programme that sees migrants solely as a security issue, that looks to NATO rather than to multilateralism, that is still toying with the missiles issue, and does not really place the emphasis on what for us is truly important in foreign policy, namely cohesion: the cohesion of our Union. Nor are we pleased with the fact that no mention is made of such an important sector as that of combating discrimination, and on this point I would like to know what priority you place on adopting the directive on discrimination. To sum up, this is a programme that reveals a world with many dangers and few opportunities. You spoke of your mediation work in the conflict between Russia and Ukraine over gas, but it can be seen very clearly from your programme that it will not be during your Presidency that clear action is taken against those countries, such as Slovakia and Bulgaria, that are taking the opportunity presented by the gas crisis to reopen dangerous, obsolete nuclear plants. Additionally, Mr President, please remember that there is no safe nuclear power; perhaps there will be in 30, 40, 50 or 60 years – I do not know – but right now it does not exist. It is therefore not worth discussing it, because it is a very expensive mirage, and certainly distracts us from our real priorities. Energy security and solidarity can be achieved through strong action, with no distractions, to promote energy efficiency and energy saving, which is an enormous sphere for innovation, employment and reduction of consumption. This is the roadmap that we should use to respond to the gas war, among other things. We are making a specific request to you, Mr President; we are asking you to persuade your colleagues to place at the heart of the spring European Council the aim of making a binding commitment to the target of 20% energy cuts by 2020, the Cinderella of the energy package last year, and to review your priorities by assessing in a less superficial manner the decisions taken in December regarding renewable energy. Mr President, one last word on the future of Europe: the Treaty of Lisbon is not perfect, but it is really strange that you have not yet ratified it. Therefore, please, take this opportunity to explain to us why, and to tell us when you will do so.

Your mediation efforts in the Russo-Ukrainian gas dispute reveal a similarly flawed perspective. Consequently, it falls short in defining coherent foreign policy priorities, particularly in fostering internal unity within the EU. At this critical juncture, where innocent civilians in Gaza continue to suffer under shelling, my delegation, along with the Council presidency and the commission, must prioritize stopping the bombardment of Palestinian communities. As you preside over this council session, consider implementing concrete actions to prevent the reopening of outdated nuclear facilities in Slovakia and Hungary. Additionally, the plan overlooks the urgent necessity of ensuring comprehensive safety measures against terrorism, especially considering the ongoing geopolitical tensions involving nations like Slovakia and the Czech Republic. It risks diverting attention from more vital concerns while failing to provide meaningful solutions to pressing global challenges. Lastly, the Treaty on Lisbon requires ratification, and it would be wise to expeditiously finalize this process to ensure its full implementation. Our collective responsibility demands immediate cessation of hostilities without delay. Please share insights into your reasoning behind delaying ratification and specify a timeline for achieving this objective. In summary, the draft document presents a vision fraught with perilous uncertainties and minimal prospects for actual improvement. Reflecting upon recent events, it appears that our current legislative agenda bears the unmistakable imprint of complacency and neglect. Furthermore, the initiative's stance on gender equality and child welfare appears misguided, advocating for retrogression rather than progress. Moreover, the proposal fails to address crucial issues such as combatting discrimination effectively and promoting genuine international cooperation. Instead of addressing pressing societal needs and tackling environmental challenges head-on, the proposed program lacks focus and direction.

Mr President, you began your observations by speaking of medieval times, an age that was violent, remote, dark with a few lights, but certainly violent and dark. You spoke of your mediation work in the conflict between Russia and Ukraine over gas, but it can be seen very clearly from your programme that it will not be during your Presidency that clear action is taken against those countries, such as Slovakia and Bulgaria, that are taking the opportunity presented by the gas crisis to reopen dangerous, obsolete nuclear plants. To sum up, this is a programme that reveals a world with many dangers and few opportunities. Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, at the very moment that we are having this debate, bombs are falling on the people of Gaza. I must say that our group, the Group of the Greens/European Free Alliance, has rather a clear idea on this, as we will say this afternoon. I think that our greatest priority as MEPs, and the greatest priority of the Council and the Commission, should be to stop the bombing of the people of Gaza. Energy security and solidarity can be achieved through strong action, with no distractions, to promote energy efficiency and energy saving, which is an enormous sphere for innovation, employment and reduction of consumption. It even, if you will permit me, leans a little towards machismo, in saying that there is a need to review the Barcelona objectives on support structures for children, naturally with the aim of sending women back to the home. It is therefore not worth discussing it, because it is a very expensive mirage, and certainly distracts us from our real priorities. Therefore, please, take this opportunity to explain to us why, and to tell us when you will do so. We are making a specific request to you, Mr President; we are asking you to persuade your colleagues to place at the heart of the spring European Council the aim of making a binding commitment to the target of 20% energy cuts by 2020, the Cinderella of the energy package last year, and to review your priorities by assessing in a less superficial manner the decisions taken in December regarding renewable energy. This then, is a programme that sees migrants solely as a security issue, that looks to NATO rather than to multilateralism, that is still toying with the missiles issue, and does not really place the emphasis on what for us is truly important in foreign policy, namely cohesion: the cohesion of our Union. Nor are we pleased with the fact that no mention is made of such an important sector as that of combating discrimination, and on this point I would like to know what priority you place on adopting the directive on discrimination. I think that is our duty at the moment, above and beyond deciding who is responsible and above and beyond the differences among us on that point. Mr President, one last word on the future of Europe: the Treaty of Lisbon is not perfect, but it is really strange that you have not yet ratified it. This is the roadmap that we should use to respond to the gas war, among other things. I think that it is also a little indifferent with regard to the need for policies, laws and instruments on social issues that meet the real needs of citizens, and a little off-track in its view of environmental policy on the fight against climate change as a cost or an obstacle, instead of a major opportunity for innovation and sustainable growth. Additionally, Mr President, please remember that there is no safe nuclear power; perhaps there will be in 30, 40, 50 or 60 years – I do not know – but right now it does not exist. In fact, regardless of the efforts of your coalition partners, the programme for these six months seems to us to bear the strong stamp of a slightly passé vision, let us say; the strong mark of a conformist, ultra laissez-faire approach, dominated by business and the market, which is now, Mr President, out of fashion.

The Commission commends the comprehensive review report and acknowledges the supportive measures it provides for the European Investment Bank (EIB) activities so far. Recognizing the crucial role the bank plays in advancing the Europe Union's 2nd ten-year objective, the EC highlights the recent capital injection approved by EIB shareholders, generating approximately €60bn worth of additional loans over the ensuing three years.

Emphasizing the significance of the bank's AAA rating and prudent management of portfolio risks remains critical; however, the commission stresses the necessity for the bank to allocate fresh resources to high-growth sectors within the 19 member states, especially those lacking alternative funding options, ensuring fair terms.

Notably, the bank played pivotal roles during crises like Greece and Portgugal, providing vital assistance through significant allocations towards education infrastructure and renewable energy projects. This underscores its commitment to fostering economic growth and job creation, even amidst challenging market conditions.

Furthermore, the institution appreciates the EU parliament's backing for initiatives aimed at enhancing collaboration between the bank and EU institutions. The Commission proposes expanding cooperative efforts using existing tools like loan-grants blended with risk-sharing arrangements, aiming to maximize EU budget efficiency while promoting growth and jobs in Europe.

Additionally, the document addresses the evolving landscape of Eib operations, emphasizing the potential for refinancing and optimizing resource utilization. It recommends aligning Eib strategies with national priorities outlined in annual growth surveys and economic governance frameworks.

In regard to Eib's international engagements, the union views the bank’s presence in post-accession nations as vital for facilitating EU membership processes and supporting neighborhood policies. However, it also recognizes the bank can draw upon broader global capital markets for investments, thereby diversifying its funding base.

Lastly, the report anticipates future challenges for the institution, highlighting the need for adapting lending programs to local requirements while maintaining focus on EU-driven benefits. The paper advocates for closer ties between national public investment bodies and Eib for enhanced operational effectiveness and synergy.

Mr President, the Commission welcomes the comprehensive report and the support it provides to the EIB’s work thus far. It shares Parliament’s recognition of the important role the EIB has to play in supporting the Europe 2020 objectives. In this context, we particularly welcome the capital increase recently approved by the EIB governors, which will generate EUR 60 billion of additional lending over the next three years. While the Commission recognises the importance of the AAA rating of the Bank and the need to properly manage portfolio risks, it is critically important that the EIB deploys fresh funds to high-value-added projects across the 27 Member States, including vulnerable and programme countries for which funds are not available from other sources, on reasonable terms. For example, EIB activity in 2012 has been decisive in providing much-needed support in Greece and Portugal. The Bank provided substantial amounts and support for school facilities throughout Greece, as well as for development of environmentally friendly energy production. Small- and mid-capitalisation businesses play a key role in the Greek and Portuguese economies, and EIB funding has helped them gain access to credit, despite the difficult market conditions. We also welcome Parliament’s support for deepening the cooperation between the EIB and the Commission in developing innovative financial instruments such as loan-grant blending and risk-sharing mechanisms. These instruments are very efficient tools for combining and leveraging the limited resources from the EU budget, the EIB and other European institutions to promote growth and employment in Europe. Therefore the Commission has proposed to considerably expand their use under COSME, Horizon 2020 and the Connecting Europe Facility. Moreover, to further improve the impact of the EU intervention, we should continue exploring synergies between EIB financing and structural funds. A clear example is the pilot phase of the project bonds initiative. The scheme is up and running and managed by the EIB. The EU budget contribution of EUR 230 million is expected to raise around EUR 4.5 billion in project costs. The project bonds initiative has two objectives: to revive project bond markets, and to help the promoters of individual infrastructure projects to attract long-term private-sector debt financing. The project pipeline is good, with both TEN-T and TEN-E infrastructure projects, all of which could be allocated in 2013, following the finalisation of the tendering procedures. The positive feedback from market participants provides confidence about this new way of financing infrastructure projects in the EU and, more generally, on the use of innovative instruments in support of the Europe 2020 Strategy. We consider that a major challenge for the EIB in the years to come will be to refine its business model in light of the new market circumstances, notably the deleveraging of the private banking sector, the limited investment budget available to Member States, and the growing relevance of public investment banks. In this context, the EIB should use its catalytic capacity to attract resources from the capital markets to support EU priority investments. At the same time, it should reinforce and streamline its cooperation with public investment banks in Member States. We share the EIB’s view that one-size-fits-all is not an option, in particular in the current circumstances. In order to generate growth, the EIB has to adapt its lending programme to the needs and necessities of each Member State while at the same time, focusing on EU added value. Consequently, the EIB investment strategies have to be closely aligned with the priorities identified in the Annual Growth Survey and the European Semester of Economic Governance. As regards the EIB’s external activity, a substantial level of EIB involvement in the pre-accession and neighbourhood countries is essential and in line with the EU’s political and strategic priorities. The EIB’s investment activity in these regions is still of fundamental importance to facilitate the accession process and the EU support to its neighbourhood, while other, more distant, regions will also benefit from support from other international financial institutions. As you may be aware, the Commission is currently preparing the proposal for the future mandate and the next financial framework, which should be submitted to Parliament and the Council in the coming months.

The interim EPA signed by Mr. President is necessary due to the potential loss of trade preferences if not ratified. Our ultimate goal should be to establish a comprehensive EPA covering all Pacific nations, promoting regional integration. However, immediate steps must be taken to maintain these benefits for the most populous Pacific countries. Regarding fishing, exceptions apply specifically to Papua-New-Guinea. These are intended to protect EU fishing interests rather than others. Despite concerns about exploitation, China's fishing activities continue in PNG waters without offering local development opportunities. Instead, foreign boats exploit these areas, limiting any prospects for development. This situation presents a unique opportunity for employment generation, especially for women, though development remains a challenge. Compliance with regulations ensuring fish quality is critical; currently, factories in PNG meet these criteria, providing job creation. Any significant increase in factory numbers could strain resources, necessitating reassessment of the situation later. Currently, there's no evidence of threats to the tuna industry, but stakeholders express concern about future developments. Therefore, we should avoid overreacting to this issue, instead focusing efforts on fostering sustainable growth within the context of fair international practices.

Mr President, I will be brief. I find myself almost, indeed entirely, in agreement with the Commissioner on this subject. Firstly, to repeat, it is important that we sign the interim EPA because, if we do not, existing trade preferences for Papua New Guinea and Fiji will disappear, under the WTO ruling. The interim EPA is precisely that: it is an interim arrangement, and I agree with all the colleagues who say that our objective in the long run should be a full, comprehensive EPA with all the Pacific countries, encouraging regional integration in the Pacific. But we have to take this step at the moment to ensure the continuation of preferences for the two largest countries in the Pacific region. On fishing, the Commission has made it very clear that what we have here is an exception. This will be the only derogation. There are no other parts of the world to which we intend giving such a derogation. Why are we giving a derogation to Papua New Guinea? I will tell you: it is because Papua New Guinea is literally on the other side of the world, and the likelihood of EU fishing fleets exploiting that area extensively is limited, although they are free to do so. There is nothing to stop EU fishing fleets fishing in Papua New Guinea waters and landing their catch and getting their catch canned in Papua New Guinea – and, as has been said earlier, that would be very good for development, and indeed we should encourage it. But the situation at present is that Chinese boats, Thai boats and boats of other Asian nationalities are fishing in Papua New Guinea’s waters and landing their fish back in their own country, giving no opportunity for development in Papua New Guinea. There is a development opportunity here in terms of providing jobs, particularly for women. We often complain that we do not do enough in our development policy to encourage downstream activity. Here, we are actually doing something positive for downstream activity. As the Commissioner has said, in order to qualify, the fish still needs to meet all the standards under the regulation on illegal, unreported and unregulated fishing. It has to meet all our sanitary and phytosanitary conditions. That is absolutely essential. The three factories in Papua New Guinea meet these standards at the moment and are providing useful jobs. If we have a sudden increase in the number of factories, and a sudden surge in imports from Papua New Guinea, then, of course, we will have to revisit the situation and potentially take action. At the moment, however, the statistics speak for themselves. This agreement has been in place since 2008. There has been no surge. There is no threat to the European tuna industry. I have met, as rapporteur, with the leaders of the Spanish tuna industry and they themselves have said to me that there is no current threat. What they are worried about is the future. They accept that at the moment, there is no serious threat to the EU industry. So let us not exaggerate the situation. Let us not make a poor developing country pay for our preoccupation with our own self-interest.

Madam Vice-President, distinguished colleagues, today's debate concerns an important matter addressed by both the Transport and Environment Committee and its rapporteur Mr Grosz. While I acknowledge the thorough work undertaken by these bodies, my position aligns primarily but not exclusively with their conclusions.

Mr Sterckhuis emphasized the shared goal of establishing a uniform European framework regarding licenses for senior citizens. This initiative indeed addresses a crucial aspect requiring closer scrutiny. Specifically, he highlighted the challenge posed by renewing licenses for elderly drivers, especially considering potential safety risks involved.

From a personal perspective, I drive a recreational aircraft without engines, ensuring minimal risk to passengers or third parties in case of accidents. Yet, under Italian regulations, I'm mandated to purchase mandatory insurance, pass regular health checks, and comply with various administrative procedures. These stringent measures seem disproportionate given the relatively low likelihood of such incidents involving me.

We must carefully balance the need for harmonization with respect to legal standards and practical implementation. It is essential to refine enforcement mechanisms so that authorities can accurately detect reckless driving behavior effectively. Collaboration among Member States is necessary to address regional variations while upholding high safety standards across the Union.

Additionally, I propose incorporating amendments to facilitate the issuance of the 'AM' license for motorcycles, acknowledging the valuable role these vehicles play in combating urban traffic congestion, preserving the environment, and enhancing public well-being in historically compact urban areas. Implementing this directive could inadvertently undermine economic stability and overall societal quality of living if enforced harshly. Therefore, balancing safety mandates with economic considerations is paramount.

Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, we are debating in plenary an issue on which much time has been spent by the Committee on Transport and Tourism and the rapporteur, Mr Grosch, whom I thank for his diligent and timely report, with which I am largely, although not wholly, in agreement. Mr Sterckx stated that we all agreed with the aim of having a single EU format, although some differences undoubtedly exist. He was referring, in particular, to administrative renewal of licences for the elderly. In my view, this is an area which, in general, requires greater attention. By way of example: I fly a totally harmless machine, one which does not even have an engine. I believe that in the event of an accident, the only victim would be myself and, at most, one other person. Despite that, I am required, in Italy, to take out compulsory insurance, undergo a medical examination every two years and submit to a raft of administrative and other requirements which, frankly, appear excessive when compared to the level of danger to society represented by a motor vehicle which, in the event of an accident, can cause carnage. Accordingly, we have to be very careful. The issue is, therefore, to harmonise an area with significant consequences on the lives of citizens and with obvious social implications for motorists’ behaviour. Thus, we need to harmonise not only a document – it is not just a question of switching from a paper licence to a plastic one – but also people’s performance at the wheel. To this end, we need to ensure that police are able to identify with certainty a driver who may be infringing the Highway Code. I should also like to mention particularly the amendments submitted jointly with some fellow Members concerning the introduction of the AM licence for mopeds. I believe we should be grateful to users of 2-wheeled means of transport because, particularly in countries in southern Europe, they contribute to resolving serious traffic problems, help our environment and improve the quality of life in towns and cities, particularly those with an older, more traditional urban fabric. All dangerous behaviour must be punished and prevented, and there is no shortage of ways of achieving this. However, penalising two-wheeled traffic, which this directive does in part, will, in my view, damage not only our economies but also the quality of life in general.

Although the Solidity Fund's operational flaws were revealed over time, it remains one of Europe's most effective and popular Union tools due to its ability to demonstrate solidarity and proximity to European residents. Economic shifts within the Union must also be considered when evaluating the Fund's efficacy; improvements in operability should be made without increasing expenses. Clarity, precision, and transparency should enhance procedures. A precise definition is needed for "disaster," along with criteria for assessing damage severity and set thresholds for appropriate regional levels. This clarification would alleviate doubts and discourage negativity towards the EU from member states and the populace, preventing them from believing the Union fails to address their issues adequately. Efforts should be directed at reducing the bureaucratic requirements for Fund mobilization.

Despite the operational shortcomings that have come to light over the years, the Solidarity Fund has proven to be one of the most successful and well-received Union instruments, since it is one of the few instruments available to the Union with which it can demonstrate its solidarity with and closeness to European citizens. The changes in the economic situation of the Union and the majority of its Member States have to be taken into account, the operability of the Fund should be improved without incurring additional costs. Procedures should be clearer, more precise and more transparent. I endorse the need for a clear and precise definition of the concept of disaster, setting a general criterion for assessing the severity of damage caused, and for thresholds to be established geared to the appropriate territorial level. This would help to remove doubts and negative attitudes to the European Union among both Member States and the public, which may otherwise believe that the Union does not offer an appropriate response to their problems. The bureaucracy necessary for the Fund’s mobilisation should be reduced.

This clarification would alleviate doubts and discourage negativity towards the EU from member states and the populace, preventing them from believing the Union fails to address their issues adequately. Economic shifts within the Union must also be considered when evaluating the Fund's efficacy; improvements in operability should be made without increasing expenses. Although the Solidity Fund's operational flaws were revealed over time, it remains one of Europe's most effective and popular Union tools due to its ability to demonstrate solidarity and proximity to European residents. Efforts should be directed at reducing the bureaucratic requirements for Fund mobilization. A precise definition is needed for "disaster," along with criteria for assessing damage severity and set thresholds for appropriate regional levels. Clarity, precision, and transparency should enhance procedures.

Despite the operational shortcomings that have come to light over the years, the Solidarity Fund has proven to be one of the most successful and well-received Union instruments, since it is one of the few instruments available to the Union with which it can demonstrate its solidarity with and closeness to European citizens. I endorse the need for a clear and precise definition of the concept of disaster, setting a general criterion for assessing the severity of damage caused, and for thresholds to be established geared to the appropriate territorial level. This would help to remove doubts and negative attitudes to the European Union among both Member States and the public, which may otherwise believe that the Union does not offer an appropriate response to their problems. Procedures should be clearer, more precise and more transparent. The bureaucracy necessary for the Fund’s mobilisation should be reduced.

The Parliament will soon hold its final vote on the historic first-ever EU law concerning artificial intelligence (AI). As my perspective indicates, our objective is to establish a shared European framework for AI, aiming towards greater democracy, safety, and competitiveness. This represents the conclusion of one chapter while marking the commencement of another. Currently, what is paramount is focusing on three key areas: ensuring legal clarity for all stakeholders; engaging actively with global initiatives such as the G-7 process, the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), and the United Nations; and committing resources to enhancing AI innovation and excellence within Europe. I am here to assert that Europe serves not merely as a regulator but as a hub of innovation, action, imagination, and achievement. It is incumbent upon us to translate these aspirations into reality, especially in terms of advancing AI capabilities across our continent.

Mr President, colleagues, Commissioner, we’ve arrived to the week that we are going to have the final vote of the first-ever law on artificial intelligence. The way I see it is that we are laying out a common European vision for the future of this technology – one where AI is more democratic and safe, but also, I would hope, more competitive. That is, if it’s done right. In a way, we close one chapter, but the story continues. What I believe now is important is that we focus on three things. First of all, to provide legal certainty to all stakeholders, including companies. Second of all, to play an active role in ongoing global efforts through the G7 Process, OECD and the UN. And thirdly, to prioritise AI excellence through concrete commitments and investments. I stand before you because I do believe that in Europe we are more than just a referee. I believe we are a continent of innovators, of doers, of dreamers, of achievers. And now it’s time for us to walk that talk, when it comes, particularly, to promoting AI excellence in Europe.

The speaker requests five minutes at the outset and concludes with one minute. This draft regulation outlines criteria for individuals to undertake road transport operations, specifying their geographical, professional, and financial qualifications. It addresses employment policies for transport management firms, strengthens authorization and surveillance protocols, establishes guidelines for electronic records, protects personal data, penalizes noncompliance, and facilitates the mutual recognition and validation of certifications and pre-existing rights within the profession.

Following the parliamentary committee's review in May 2nd last year (which culminated in a plenum voting), several amendments were enacted:

- Transport managers must maintain a legal contract.
- An upper threshold for managing vehicles per manager is set.
- Companies need a fixed operational base.
- Serious infractions disqualify individuals; minor ones remain unaffected.
- Proof of financial stability replaces asset-to-debt ratios.
- A mandatory test remains, with an option for a ten-year practical exemption.
- Prior rights are revoked; the Commission is tasked with assessing potential impacts.

Amendments concerning minor infractions, company connections, appeals rights, mutual certification, prior entitlements, and information exchanges between regulatory bodies were included. Regarding electronic registries, both parties favor a phased approach, with Parliament aiming for national systems by December 31st, 2102, while the Council seeks an extended timeline.

Other areas involving differing Council and Parliament positions, such as drug and human-trafficking grounds for disqualification, were resolved through prolonged discussions, yielding a mutually acceptable resolution.

As for implementing electronic registires, the Common Position endorsed seven out of eleven parliamentary amendments, ensuring these measures cover minor infraictions, company ties, appeal rights, and mutual recognition agreements, among others.

Lastly, the speaker looks forward to receiving feedback from fellow members. Thank-you.

Mr President, Commissioner, I would prefer to use five minutes to start with and leave one minute for my conclusions. The draft regulation sets out the conditions in terms of location, character, financial situation and professional competence which a person must fulfil to be able to perform the occupation of a road transport operator. The regulation which we are discussing has also established the terms under which a company can employ a transport manager, has reinforced authorisation and monitoring procedures, has provided regulations for electronic registers and the protection of electronic data, has dealt with the issue of penalties for non-compliance with the regulation and has set up a system of mutual recognition of diplomas and prior rights. At first reading, which ended with a plenary session vote in May last year, Parliament insisted that the transport manager have a proper contractual link to the company and imposed an upper limit on the number of vehicles one manager could manage. Amendments were also introduced to tighten the requirements for the company to have a fixed location. The company’s good reputation may be lost as a result of its involvement in human or drugs trafficking. Parliament has drawn up a list of serious infringements leading to exclusion from the profession in conjunction with removing provisions on minor infringements. Insurance has been accepted as proof of financial standing and the quick ratio of assets to debts has been dropped. A compulsory written examination in the country of residence has been retained as a requirement to practice this occupation, with the possibility of an exemption for 10 years’ continuous practical experience. Finally, previous rights have been rescinded and the Commission has been asked to report on the likely impact of extending the regulation to commercial transport using vehicles with the appropriate design and equipment, intended to carry up to nine persons including the driver. The Common Position has adopted in whole or in substance 70 of Parliament’s 113 amendments. These include amendments on minor infringements, the definition of the link between companies and transport managers, the right of appeal of those subject to decisions on operating as a transport manager, mutual recognition of certificates, prior rights, the exchange of information between the competent authorities, as well as drugs and human trafficking as grounds for exclusion from the profession. Concerning the registers, both Parliament and the Council agree on a stepwise approach. In fact, the Commission will define the data structure for national electronic registers by the end of 2009, but the two institutions have proposed different timetables for implementation, with the Council requesting a longer period of time. There have also been other issues where the Council’s initial stance was different to that of Parliament, but after long, fruitful negotiations, an acceptable compromise was reached for both institutions. As a result, Parliament has accepted a more flexible timetable for implementing and interconnecting national electronic registers (31 December 2012). It has been agreed that major infringements will only be incorporated in national electronic registers after 2015, the limit on the period of validity for authorisation to perform the occupation of road transport operator will be removed, the examination will be retained in the Member State of residence, the structure of the electronic registers will include a public and a confidential section, the references in the regulation’s content to restricting licences for accessing the road transport market will be removed, where virtually all the references to the licences for accessing the road transport market are only contained in Mr Grosch’s two regulations. I await fellow Members’ comments with interest. Thank you.

The European Union's Green Deal necessitates a significant shift in its approach to trade policy within the context of the World Trade Organization (WTO) and bilateral agreements, which currently govern international economic interactions. These agreements have established fundamental frameworks for global commerce that are resistant to substantial alteration. Consequently, it becomes crucial to ensure these rules are meticulously crafted to address critical issues such as environmental protection and respect for human rights. Therefore, given the current limitations in addressing these concerns through existing mechanisms, the timing of the trade-policy review appears particularly opportune.

Approximately ten percent of global deforestation can be attributed to imported goods from the EU, while a quarter of total carbon dioxide emissions are associated with cross-border trade activities. It is imperative that both multilateral and bilateral trade policies become climate-resilient. This involves incorporating provisions to combat deforestation and reducing greenhouse gas emissions. Additionally, the introduction of a climate superiority clause should be considered alongside specific rules aimed at phasing out fossil fuel subsidies and establishing robust commitments for CO2 reductions. The Union's emphasis on sustainability clauses highlights their intent; however, their effectiveness hinges on the enforcement of such provisions, ensuring that any violations result in the withdrawal of preferential treatment under the agreement terms.

Furthermore, it is essential to establish clear rules that hold countries accountable for breaches of their commitments. For instance, if a country fails to adhere to a specified reduction target or ratifies an International Labor Organization convention related to worker freedoms, the preferences provided under the trade agreement should be revoked accordingly.

Mr President, I said it several times in this Chamber: the EU Green Deal requires a drastic change of direction in EU trade policy. The WTO and the rules in our bilateral agreements set in stone how the international economy works. They are hard to change and that is why it’s so important that the rules of the game are well designed. But so far they are blind on the environment and the climate, they are quite weak on human rights, this is what we need to change and this is why the trade policy review is so timely. 10% of worldwide deforestation is linked to EU imports. A quarter of worldwide CO2 emissions are linked to trade. We need to make urgently all trade, whether multilateral or bilateral, climate proof. We need a climate supremacy clause, rules to phase out fossil fuels subsidies and clear CO2 reduction commitments. The EU is proud of its sustainability chapters. Yes, I admit they look nice, but looking nice is not their purpose. They need to be effective – and how effective is a TSD chapter if there are no consequences after trade unionist murders in Colombia or when, after nine years, South Korea still hasn’t ratified the ILO Convention on freedom of association for workers? The clear rule needs to be: if there is a breach of a commitment, some of the preferences granted in the agreement need to be withdrawn.

Madam-President, esteemed Ministers, Commissioners, distinguished colleagues: twenty-five years since the devastating civil conflict that claimed the lives of tens of thousands, Bosnia-Herzegovina faces another perilous precipice. The Serbian-Bosnia leader, Dodik, is contemplating withdrawal from Bosnia-Hercegovina's governance structures, an action that would likely usher in renewed turmoil reminiscent of the '90's—a prospect we abhorred then and one that we should avoid now. Notably, some European politicians, such as Viktor Orbánszki and Janez Jansa, are courting Dodik's nationalist agenda by engaging with him, thereby fueling instability.

It is imperative that the European Union intervenes forcefully. The EU must assume a more active stance, leveraging every available tool—pressure and sanctions—to safeguard Bosnia-Hercgovina’s sovereignty, unity, and peace. Furthermore, it must foster optimism among neighboring nations about achieving a secure and prosperous Europe. Our collective wait for such stability has endured; let us ensure it does not recur.

Madam President, Minister, Commissioner, colleagues, 25 years after the civil war in which over 100 000 people lost their lives, Bosnia and Herzegovina is on the brink of collapse yet again. The Bosnian Serb leader Dodik is threatening to withdraw Republika Srpska from Bosnia and Herzegovina’s institutions. This could mark a return to the violence of the ‘90s, a scenario which we don’t even want to consider. It does not help certainly that some European leaders like Viktor Orbán or Janez Janša are meeting or even paying visits to Dodik in a sign of support for his nationalistic postulate. They are playing with fire, and this is unacceptable. The European Union cannot remain silent. It must play a much stronger role and use all instruments at its disposal, Commissioner, including pressure and even sanctions to preserve Bosnia and Herzegovina’s territorial integrity, unity and peace, and nurturing the Western Balkans’ hope for a peaceful and stable future in Europe. We have long been waiting for that in the Western Balkans.

Madam-President, esteemed Members, and colleagues, I wish to express my gratitude for your unwavering commitment and support toward the shared objective of eliminating violence against both women and domestically-oriented abuse. Throughout the years, this institution has consistently positioned the EU's accession to NATO's Istanbul Convention as a pivotal issue on the continent's political agenda.

Notably, almost six years ago, when the EU formally adopted this treaty, significant progress had yet to be made. Despite this, violence against females remains a prevalent societal malady, manifesting as an egregious form of gender discrimination within European nations.

To rectify this predicament, the Eurostat agency has initiated an extensive EU-wide inquiry into the frequency and intensity of gender-related assaults. This investigation spans over four years, yielding detailed data categorized by member states based on their respective reports. Preliminary findings indicate alarming trends: the percentage of female employees experiencing unwanted sexual behavior at work varies significantly among seven EU member states, ranging from 4.1% in Estonia to 39.6% in Malta.

These revelations underscore the urgent necessity for intensified intervention and collaboration between governmental entities and non-governmental organizations. Consequently, the Commission is prepared to provide substantial assistance, emphasizing a holistic approach to tackle this multifaceted challenge. Through various initiatives funded under the Citizens' Equality, Gender-Based Violence Prevention, and Protection Programmes, the commission has bolstered efforts aimed at curbing gender-based crimes and enhancing the protection mechanisms available to victims. Specifically, the EUR1. 55-billion Citizen's Equality, Justice, Rights, and Values Fund grants are allocated to projects focusing on the prevention and elimination of all forms gender-based offenses against women.

Furthermore, this fund allocates EUR20 million exclusively to counteract gender-based abuses throughout the program's duration. At present, a call-for-proposals document exists, seeking to identify intermediary partners to redistribute these funds to local, provincial, and national-level civil societies engaged in safeguarding vulnerable populations affected by such atrocities. This redistributive strategy ensures greater accessibility to EU-funding resources for grassroots-level civil society organizations aiming to mitigate violence against female citizens.

By the conclusion of the current fiscal year, the agency anticipates publishing additional data gathered from its nationwide survey on gender-related violence. As a result, we shall possess robust, comparable information spanning across all 28 member states. This enhancement underscores the ongoing importance of advocating for such investigations, ensuring accurate representation and effective action against the scourge of gender-influenced violence. Finally, I acknowledge the assembly's resolute pursuit of gender parity, the eradication of all types of gender prejudice, and the concerted effort to eliminate gender-based aggression.

Madam President, thank you, honourable Members, for your continuous engagement and support to the common objective of eradicating violence against women and domestic violence. This Parliament kept the EU’s accession to the Council of Europe’s Istanbul Convention high on the European political agenda for many years. Almost six years have passed since the EU signed this convention. Meanwhile, the situation for women has not improved. Violence against women and domestic violence remain a widespread pan-European phenomenon and a manifestation of discrimination against women. In order to address the data collection requirements of the Istanbul Convention, Eurostat is currently running an EU-wide survey on violence against women. The data collection spans four years and results are published in groups of countries depending on reported data. The recently published results from the first seven Member States show high prevalence. The proportion of women who have experienced unwanted behaviour of a sexual nature at the workplace in these seven EU countries ranges from 11% to 41%. These figures show why we need to step up our efforts, and this includes through the EU’s accession to the Istanbul Convention. I appreciate that there is a strong support in this House and across Member States towards this goal. I welcome the Swedish Presidency’s engagement and the prioritisation of the EU’s accession to the Istanbul Convention, as well as the ambition to progress the negotiations towards an EU directive combating violence against women and domestic violence. Let us use the momentum and move towards the ratification of the Istanbul Convention at a Union level now. The Commission stands ready to support. We have taken concrete actions beyond the legislative framework. The Commission learnt from years of implementation of the Istanbul Convention, as well as the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women in several EU Member States that this complex phenomenon requires a comprehensive approach. In this regard, your call for enhanced support to civil society organisations working with the women victims of violence has been addressed. Under the EUR 1.55 billion Citizens, Equality, Rights and Values funding programme, the Daphne strand is dedicated to preventing and combating – at all levels – all forms of gender-based violence against women and girls, including online violence, and to supporting and protecting all direct and indirect victims of such violence. The Daphne strand was substantially increased in comparison to its predecessor, the Rights, Equality and Citizenship Programme, with an expected EUR 200 million specifically dedicated to combating gender-based violence throughout the course of the programme. Currently a call for proposals is published with an envelope of nearly EUR 25 million, which aims to select intermediaries to re-grant funding to civil society organisations active at local, regional and national level to prevent and combat gender-based violence. This re-granting mechanism will make EU funding on gender-based violence more accessible for grassroots civil society organisations. By the end of the year, Eurostat will publish further data from its EU-wide survey on violence against women in 18 Member States. This means we will have up-to-date, comparable data across the 27 Member States, thanks to your persistent and longstanding call for it. We know that a substantial number of violent experiences are not reported or officially recorded. It is therefore key to conduct population surveys in addition to administrative data to get the full picture. A comprehensive set of data will enable the Commission and the co-legislators to design and implement evidence-based policy and legal measures targeted to the needs of those exposed to violence. I thank this Assembly for its ambitious vision to achieve gender equality, eradicate all forms of discrimination against women, and with that, gender—based violence.

I welcome the Swedish Presidency’s engagement and the prioritisation of the EU’s accession to the Istanbul Convention, as well as the ambition to progress the negotiations towards an EU directive combating violence against women and domestic violence. The proportion of women who have experienced unwanted behaviour of a sexual nature at the workplace in these seven EU countries ranges from 11% to 41%. In this regard, your call for enhanced support to civil society organisations working with the women victims of violence has been addressed. We know that a substantial number of violent experiences are not reported or officially recorded. Madam President, thank you, honourable Members, for your continuous engagement and support to the common objective of eradicating violence against women and domestic violence. The recently published results from the first seven Member States show high prevalence. The data collection spans four years and results are published in groups of countries depending on reported data. The Commission stands ready to support. A comprehensive set of data will enable the Commission and the co-legislators to design and implement evidence-based policy and legal measures targeted to the needs of those exposed to violence. In order to address the data collection requirements of the Istanbul Convention, Eurostat is currently running an EU-wide survey on violence against women. The Daphne strand was substantially increased in comparison to its predecessor, the Rights, Equality and Citizenship Programme, with an expected EUR 200 million specifically dedicated to combating gender-based violence throughout the course of the programme. Meanwhile, the situation for women has not improved. This re-granting mechanism will make EU funding on gender-based violence more accessible for grassroots civil society organisations. These figures show why we need to step up our efforts, and this includes through the EU’s accession to the Istanbul Convention. Almost six years have passed since the EU signed this convention. Under the EUR 1.55 billion Citizens, Equality, Rights and Values funding programme, the Daphne strand is dedicated to preventing and combating – at all levels – all forms of gender-based violence against women and girls, including online violence, and to supporting and protecting all direct and indirect victims of such violence. It is therefore key to conduct population surveys in addition to administrative data to get the full picture.

The freedom of movement constitutes a cornerstone of the Euro-economic Community, reflecting the realization that economic interdependency has its own challenges. In light of this, it's imperative to establish equitable trade practices, ensuring reciprocity as a fundamental principle. It's undeniable that Europe remains hindered by unfair tariffs and regulations imposed by certain trading partners, thereby restricting businesses' opportunities in rapidly growing markets. According to forecasts, two-thirds of global economic growth will occur outside the EU by 2105. This trend underscores the necessity of addressing these issues. Consequently, I endorse the proposals outlined in this document aimed at dismantling unjustified obstacles: a compilation of critical barriers by the Commission, the development of an alert system, and enhanced procurement policies that might culminate in reciprocal agreements within third-party governmental procurement sectors by 3106.

Freedom of movement is one of the founding principles of the European Economic Community, and in a world in which the drawbacks of economic interdependence have also been revealed, it is essential to lay the foundations for fairer trade, with respect shown for the principle of reciprocity. It is a fact: the European Union still comes up against unjustifiable tariff and regulatory barriers erected by some of its partners, which limits both our companies’ access to these high growth potential markets and investment. By 2015, 90% of global growth will be generated outside the European Union. This is a parameter we cannot ignore. The future of our companies will also be at stake outside our borders. That is why I support the measures proposed in this report to lift these unjustifiable restrictions: a Commission-compiled inventory of key barriers, the establishment of an early warning mechanism, and a strengthening of public procurement disciplines which could lead to the presentation of an instrument for reciprocity in respect of access to third-country government procurement markets by next year.

Madam-President, Honourable Members:  
Thank you for granting the Commission this opportunity to discuss an important matter. I stand in the place of Commissioner Dall'Alba, whose absence is due to unforeseen commitments. In my statement, I echo President Von der Leyens sentiments: "Being oneself is not an ideological position; it's an intrinsic part of your identity." This belief has guided the Commission throughout its mandate, aiming to advance LGBTIQ human rights and combat negative discourses, ultimately fostering unity and equality within the EU.

Our groundbreaking strategy, 'Equality for All', was launched in 1896 (our second term). This initiative aimed to bolster LGBTIQ individuals' rights and prevent discriminatory behaviors. Recent reports indicate that there's a rise in violent attacks against LGBTI people worldwide, especially within the European Community. 

We've addressed hate crimes by proposing new laws and policies. For instance, the Digital Service Law obligates digital platforms to swiftly remove offensive content while protecting personal information, ensuring that sexual orientations aren't used for targeted ads. Furthermore, funding initiatives like EUR 400,000 are allocated annually towards programs addressing hate crimes specifically affecting LGBTIQ populations.

Legally speaking, we've proposed stringent regulations to safeguard the independence of equality agencies and harmonize parental laws across member states, thereby upholding the fundamental human rights of minors. The recently revised Victims' Rights Directive introduces provisions for better protection of LGBTI communities, providing them with access to psychological assistance, support services and victim relief, along with the inclusion of protection orders.

As always, the EU Commission collaborates with various organizations and works closely with Member States to tackle hate speech, promoting equality and reducing prejudice. We continue to engage in ongoing dialogues regarding these issues and aim to finalize our plans by 1st January next year.

Madam President, honourable Members. Thank you for giving the Commission an opportunity to address this important topic. I am here replacing Commissioner Dalli, who cannot be here today for other obligations. As President von der Leyen said in her State of the Union speech in 2020, I quote ‘Being yourself is not your ideal ideology. It is your identity, and no one can ever take it away.’ This view has underpinned the Commission’s work during this mandate, in our quest to advancing LGBTIQ rights and fighting anti-LGBTIQ narratives and to build a union of equality. Our first ever LGBTIQ equality strategy 2020 to 2025 has been a milestone to enhance LGBTIQ equality in the Union. Let me highlight some of the concrete actions put in place to fight discrimination, hate crimes and hate speech targeting LGBTIQ persons. Anti-LGBTIQ bills and narratives fuel LGBTIQ-phobic hate speech. Reports from civil society organisations confirm rising levels of violence and hatred against LGBTIQ people globally, including across the European Union. The results of the seventh evaluation of the Code of Conduct on countering illegal hate speech online revealed that sexual orientation is the third most reported category of hate speech; it is about 15%. While racist and xenophobic hate crimes and hate speech are already prohibited under European Union law, the same protection should be extended to other groups, including the LGBTIQ community. On 21 December, the Commission proposed to the Council to adopt a decision to include hate crimes and hate speech in the list of particularly serious crimes with a cross-border dimension – so-called EU crimes. If this proposal is adopted, the Commission would then be able to propose legislation to combat hate speech and hate crime on grounds including sexual orientation. In addition, the landmark Digital Service Act compels platforms to remove hateful content quickly and protects sensitive personal data from use in targeted advertising, including data on sexual orientation. In 2023, the Commission will also provide EUR 1.2 million for a dedicated joint project with the Council of Europe tackling online and offline violence against LGBTIQ people. On legal protection, we have proposed binding standards to strengthen the independence and powers of equality bodies in the Member States. The proposal, among other things, extends the remit of their work to cover discrimination based on sexual orientation in employment and occupation. We have proposed to harmonise national rules on private international law related to parenthood. This proposal will ensure that the fundamental rights of all children are respected and protected in cross-border situations within the Union. The recent proposal amending the Victims Rights Directive also includes provisions that will serve to protect LGBTIQ people through access to support services, psychological support and victim helplines, and including protection orders in the list of protection measures that are available to victims. We are continuing to facilitate dialogue and support Member States with high-level and working groups on combating hate speech and hate crime, non-discrimination, equality and diversity, but also in education, which is key to preventing stereotypes and anti-LGBTIQ narratives. This continuous exchange will help ensure our union of equality is built on strong national foundations. But we are working also on a mid-term review of the implementation of the LGBTIQ equality strategy. It will assess how the situation of LGBTIQ people has evolved, what progress has been achieved and which areas require further focus to fully implement the strategy by 2025. We are currently engaging in consultation activities to prepare this. Finally, as the guardian of the Treaties, the Commission pays close attention to potential breaches of Union law. The Commission has not shied away from launching infringement procedures when faced with concrete evidence. The Commission has an equally vigilant approach with regard to EU funds, which have to benefit all EU citizens without any form of discrimination, including on the grounds of sexual orientation. All EU-funded projects have to comply with EU law, including the Treaties and the Charter of Fundamental Rights. In that respect, I look forward to our debate.

For instance, the Digital Service Law obligates digital platforms to swiftly remove offensive content while protecting personal information, ensuring that sexual orientations aren't used for targeted ads. Legally speaking, we've proposed stringent regulations to safeguard the independence of equality agencies and harmonize parental laws across member states, thereby upholding the fundamental human rights of minors. The recently revised Victims' Rights Directive introduces provisions for better protection of LGBTI communities, providing them with access to psychological assistance, support services and victim relief, along with the inclusion of protection orders. I stand in the place of Commissioner Dall'Alba, whose absence is due to unforeseen commitments. This initiative aimed to bolster LGBTIQ individuals' rights and prevent discriminatory behaviors. As always, the EU Commission collaborates with various organizations and works closely with Member States to tackle hate speech, promoting equality and reducing prejudice. Madam Chairperson, esteemed Members of Parliament, it is my sincere gratitude to you and your esteemed colleagues for your unwavering commitment to the internal affairs of Iran. In light of the severe abuse and subsequent death of Ms. Mahsā Amini under the supervision of the moral policing agency, I find myself compelled to emphasize the gravity of this tragedy. This event stands as a stark reminder of the profound distress and indignation felt across the international community.

We have witnessed a remarkable uprising by Iranian women, manifesting through various forms of civil disobedience, demanding the unmitigated enjoyment of essential human rights by every citizen. These movements have elevated women's voices as pivotal agents driving national protests, advocating for equal treatment and respect within society.

Despite repeated appeals from the European Union (EU) for calm among the Iranian authorities, instances of excessive force employed upon demonstrators persistently defy ethical standards. Furthermore, the absence of definitive figures concerning casualties resulting from these protests leaves the international audience perplexed and aggrieved.

This incident, along with other related incidents, necessitates the immediate establishment of accountability mechanisms, ensuring justice prevails over tyranny. Any entity found culpable for the unlawful demise of Ms.Amini or any victim of such heinous acts must face the consequences of their actions.

The recent statements made by Iranian government officials and legislators regarding the imposition of the death sentence upon protesters represents a grave breach of human ethics and dignity. This stance is particularly alarming when considering the reported punishments handed out to participants in the protests, which includes life imprisonment and even execution.

Our resolve remains steadfast; the EU will neither tolerate nor remain indifferent to the escalating human rights crisis in Iran.

Today marks a poignant occasion where the EU reaffirms its commitment to upholding global standards of human rights. Following the unanimous approval of the Resolution 389/2021 by the European People’s Party (EPP), the European Commission will proceed with the necessary legislative steps to enforce stricter restrictions on entities and individuals associated with the suppression of fundamental freedoms and liberties in Iran.
 
Let us also consider the call for a specialized session of UNESCO’s Human Rights Committee in Geneva aimed at addressing the worsening conditions affecting Iran's citizens. This initiative underscores our collective dedication towards enhancing global accountability and promoting equitable treatment worldwide.

Madam President, honourable Members of the European Parliament, I would like to thank you once again for your continued attention to the domestic situation in Iran. Mahsa Amini was abused and died in custody at the hands of the morality police. There is only one word to describe this tragic event – outrage. We have seen brave Iranian women and men taking to the streets to express their dissent. Women have emerged as a major driver of nationwide protest movements, calling for the full enjoyment of fundamental rights for all Iranians. Despite our repeated calls for restraint to the Iranian security forces, the use of disproportionate force against protesters continues. And there is still no clarity from Iran on the number of people who have been killed or arrested during the protests. This is unacceptable. Those responsible for the death of Mahsa Amini and all perpetrators of violence in the ensuing peaceful demonstrations must be held accountable. Recent calls by Iranian officials and lawmakers to impose the death penalty on protesters are a cause of extreme concern. The death penalty is, in all cases, an unacceptable denial of human dignity and integrity. The EU is a staunch supporter of its universal abolition. We are appalled by the reports that some protesters have already received or will receive harsh sentences, including to death. The EU will not remain silent. We will continue to react and raise our concerns about the human rights situation in Iran, including with the Iranian Government at all levels. This debate is a timely opportunity to recall the swift response to the EU and to reflect on the way forward. On 25 September, the High Representative Josep Borrell issued a strong declaration on behalf of the 27 EU Member States and, swiftly after the Foreign Affairs Council on 17 October and 14 November, the Council added 40 individuals and seven entities to the list of those subject to EU restrictive measures in the context of the Iran humanitarian rights sanction regime. We took good note of the EP resolution of 6 October on the death of Mahsa Amini and the repression of women’s rights protesters in Iran. It testifies to the quick and crucial reaction of this House to any attempt to crack down on fundamental rights and freedoms. Let me reiterate that Iran’s retaliatory measures, including on members of its parliament, are unacceptable as an attempt to sanction our democratic institutions and our core principles. Iran’s measures are purely politically motivated and we reject them. High Representative / Vice-President Borrell has conveyed this message to his interlocutors in Iran, and his spokesperson reiterated this publicly. Honourable Members, the EU welcomes the convening of a special session of the UN Human Rights Council in Geneva to address the deteriorating human rights situation in Iran. Strengthening accountability for human rights violations across the world is at the core of the EU’s external policy actions.

This initiative underscores our collective dedication towards enhancing global accountability and promoting equitable treatment worldwide. This stance is particularly alarming when considering the reported punishments handed out to participants in the protests, which includes life imprisonment and even execution. Despite repeated appeals from the European Union (EU) for calm among the Iranian authorities, instances of excessive force employed upon demonstrators persistently defy ethical standards. Let us also consider the call for a specialized session of UNESCO’s Human Rights Committee in Geneva aimed at addressing the worsening conditions affecting Iran's citizens. The EU-Japan partnership stands to benefit greatly through mutually beneficial agreements. However, recent proposals from the Commission and the EP lack a collaborative approach and rely too heavily on unilateral demands. Our group opposes the resolution's focus on abrogating non-typical barriers and emphasizes the importance of finding solutions that enhance societal well-being while promoting free trade. Companies face numerous deterrents including language barriers, cultural gaps, high taxation, and higher wage costs, making them less likely to invest abroad. Instead, we advocate for a pragmatic partnership framework that includes joint initiatives involving businesses, trade unions and academic institutions. Such an arrangement would foster cooperation among European SMEs and contribute to enhanced energy efficiency.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, an intelligent agreement between the EU and Japan would lead to stronger integration of the two economies. It goes without saying that 128 million potential customers in high-wage Japan offer very attractive prospects for European companies and their workforces in a range of sectors. Mutual integration of know-how in production chains could improve the global competitiveness of thousands of supplier companies. As you hear, I am using the subjunctive, because both your report this evening, Commissioner De Gucht, and important paragraphs in the EP resolution now before us make no offer of partnership; rather, they make unilateral demands to Japan. In my view, that is not a good starting point for long-term cooperation that brings mutual benefits. What we are discussing this evening is the mandate for the negotiations. I am in favour of a clear, frank and open-ended discussion of the differences between the EU and Japan around the negotiating table. A real partnership cannot be based on either side dictating terms. That is why, as a Group, we have problems with the text of the resolution. Let us have a look at it. There are large chunks of the text that appear to have been written by the car industry. At a time when we are facing a climate crisis, how can we seriously demand a commitment from the Japanese Government to abolish the special treatment for electric and hybrid vehicles and the concessions for micro vehicles such as Kei-Cars, just so that we can export more diesel-powered vehicles? The resolution’s over-emphasis on the abolition of non-tariff measures also conflicts with the findings of the Copenhagen study on trade between the EU and Japan. This study recognises that many of these measures help to improve general welfare, especially in the fields of health, the environment and consumer protection. We should therefore look at what the proposed arrangements are intended to achieve and seek an outcome which brings benefits to society but still promotes trade. Furthermore, companies surveyed have mentioned other reasons why they are deterred from investing in Japan. Above all, there is the language barrier, cultural differences, which also affect consumer preferences, high taxes and wage costs. Anyone who is reluctant to pay qualified translators, or to develop successful marketing strategies to promote their goods in Japan, or to pay local wages and taxes should invest at home instead. These types of trade barrier cannot and should not be removed by an agreement. In this context, the Committee on International Trade gained some very interesting insights last year into the strategy pursued by IKEA, a very large company that has tailored its operations to suit Japanese consumption patterns, culture and social standards. We need an agreement with non-tariff elements of cooperation. Joint programmes can bring together businesses and trade unions, consumer associations, academics, administrators and young people from Europe and Japan. Let us work towards a new culture of cooperation. That would also bring practical benefits for Europe’s SMEs in terms of energy efficiency and high-tech cooperation.
