The upcoming Council meetings focus on several key issues critical to the future direction of the EU. First, the revision to the MultiAnnual Financial Framework (MAFF) aims to bolster support for Ukraine's fight against Russian aggression, enhance border security, address migration challenges, boost economic competitiveness, and ensure long-term stability. This initiative underscores the importance of aligning resources with strategic priorities.

Secondly, the process of enlarging the EU remains crucial, especially considering the ongoing conflict in Ukraine. Concrete military assistance is necessary alongside financial aid to equip Ukraine effectively in its defense efforts. Additionally, the EU should demonstrate leadership in diplomatic relations by ensuring that regional powers recognize the value of EU support in stabilizing neighboring regions and promoting reforms.

Lastly, the accession process for Ukraine and Moldova represents a vital opportunity for mutual benefit. These negotiations aim to enhance the safety and stability of the region, facilitate political and economic reforms, and align domestic policies with European standards and legislation. As such, they are seen as advantageous for both parties involved.

Madam President, President von der Leyen, dear colleagues, just over the course of the recent weeks, we as institutions of the European Union have achieved a lot together: we have reached an agreement on the first global Artificial Intelligence Act, we have adopted the Critical Raw Materials Act, reducing our dependency on China and providing predictability to our industry. All of this makes Europe stronger and safer and we have, just two weeks ago here in the plenary of Parliament, voted the budget of the European Union for next year, which also gives predictability to the beneficiaries of EU funds for next year. But we need to give predictability to those who expect European support in the long term, and this is why the first important topic of this Council will be the revision of the Multiannual Financial Framework. It was agreed in 2020. Since then, we had to do many things which we did not anticipate. The Commission was right to put forward a revision proposal of the Multiannual Financial Framework in June. We the Parliament have expressed our view, and we urge the Council to adopt its position on this revision at the Council meeting this week, so that early next year we can conclude the final negotiations between Parliament and the Council on this topic. Why is this important and what do we expect? This revision is important to provide support to Ukraine, to better protect our borders, to manage migration, to strengthen our economy and make it more competitive, and indeed to support Ukraine. This revision is also important to create reserves for unforeseen developments so that, if anything unexpected happens until 2027, the Union can support people in case of need. This is the first important topic of the Council. The second one is indeed enlargement. Let me be clear: what Ukraine needs from us now is those concrete tools that help it win the war on the ground, the military support. But the financial support is also important. I was in Washington last week. The EUR 50 billion that the European Union will put forward for Ukraine will be essential to mobilise international, including US support. We cannot expect other regions of the world to support Ukraine financially and militarily unless we are there in the front line and support ourselves. Last point: the accession talks. No one is talking now about the immediate accession of Ukraine and the Republic of Moldova to the European Union, but what is important is that these negotiations start. Why is this important? Because the accession negotiations are the best tool that we, as the European Union, have to make our neighbouring countries safer and more stable, to help them reform, modernise and get closer to European standards, as well as adapt to and implement European legislation. This is why these accession negotiations are not only in the interests of our neighbouring countries, but they are in the interest of the European Union as a whole. This is the view of Parliament, of the majority of its Members. We hope the Council can unanimously make this decision this week.

Additionally, the EU should demonstrate leadership in diplomatic relations by ensuring that regional powers recognize the value of EU support in stabilizing neighboring regions and promoting reforms. As such, they are seen as advantageous for both parties involved. First, the revision to the MultiAnnual Financial Framework (MAFF) aims to bolster support for Ukraine's fight against Russian aggression, enhance border security, address migration challenges, boost economic competitiveness, and ensure long-term stability. Secondly, the process of enlarging the EU remains crucial, especially considering the ongoing conflict in Ukraine. These negotiations aim to enhance the safety and stability of the region, facilitate political and economic reforms, and align domestic policies with European standards and legislation. Lastly, the accession process for Ukraine and Moldova represents a vital opportunity for mutual benefit. The upcoming Council meetings focus on several key issues critical to the future direction of the EU. This initiative underscores the importance of aligning resources with strategic priorities. Concrete military assistance is necessary alongside financial aid to equip Ukraine effectively in its defense efforts.

Because the accession negotiations are the best tool that we, as the European Union, have to make our neighbouring countries safer and more stable, to help them reform, modernise and get closer to European standards, as well as adapt to and implement European legislation. Why is this important and what do we expect? I was in Washington last week. This is the view of Parliament, of the majority of its Members. No one is talking now about the immediate accession of Ukraine and the Republic of Moldova to the European Union, but what is important is that these negotiations start. The second one is indeed enlargement. We cannot expect other regions of the world to support Ukraine financially and militarily unless we are there in the front line and support ourselves. This is the first important topic of the Council. We the Parliament have expressed our view, and we urge the Council to adopt its position on this revision at the Council meeting this week, so that early next year we can conclude the final negotiations between Parliament and the Council on this topic. Why is this important? This revision is important to provide support to Ukraine, to better protect our borders, to manage migration, to strengthen our economy and make it more competitive, and indeed to support Ukraine. The EUR 50 billion that the European Union will put forward for Ukraine will be essential to mobilise international, including US support. Madam President, President von der Leyen, dear colleagues, just over the course of the recent weeks, we as institutions of the European Union have achieved a lot together: we have reached an agreement on the first global Artificial Intelligence Act, we have adopted the Critical Raw Materials Act, reducing our dependency on China and providing predictability to our industry. Let me be clear: what Ukraine needs from us now is those concrete tools that help it win the war on the ground, the military support. It was agreed in 2020. The Commission was right to put forward a revision proposal of the Multiannual Financial Framework in June. We hope the Council can unanimously make this decision this week. Since then, we had to do many things which we did not anticipate. Last point: the accession talks. This revision is also important to create reserves for unforeseen developments so that, if anything unexpected happens until 2027, the Union can support people in case of need. But we need to give predictability to those who expect European support in the long term, and this is why the first important topic of this Council will be the revision of the Multiannual Financial Framework. But the financial support is also important. This is why these accession negotiations are not only in the interests of our neighbouring countries, but they are in the interest of the European Union as a whole. All of this makes Europe stronger and safer and we have, just two weeks ago here in the plenary of Parliament, voted the budget of the European Union for next year, which also gives predictability to the beneficiaries of EU funds for next year.

The supporters of Lisbon's Treaty highlight the elimination of the democratic gap within the Union. However, the initial year demonstrates that the EP must struggle against the Council & Commission merely to secure parliamentary co-decision and democratic oversight. Co-decision on all fiscal matters constitutes a minimal democratic baseline. As elected to realize the European vision and balance national egoism, our duty is to protect this role. We cannot allow the EP's involvement in budgetary negotiations pertaining to the 13-20 budget framework to be compromised. New responsibilities cannot be funded through budget reallocation, especially sparingly. Faced with tight Member State budgets, it's inexplicable that the council refuses to address the matter of own resources. The EC budget should represent genuine European solidarity. Without such solidarity, we risk losing respect among European citizens. Net contributors have forgotten these principles. Despite profitable activities on the EU's internal market by some companies, Germany &amp several other nations appear miserly towards collective challenges across Europe. New definitions of solidarity have emerged where aid is contingent upon personal gain. German banks' entanglement in Ireland's financial crisis necessitates a similar level of assistance for both parties. Over the past few years, our group has opposed the EU’s budget. Our priorities concern environmental and societal progress, and insufficient attention has thus been given to reducing economic disparities. These issues deepen within the union and member states, resulting in intolerable levels of inequality. Major initiatives like ITER should be funded alongside projects providing direct support to students, jobless individuals, rural regions, and local community development. In summary, it would be prudent for us, as a pan-European perspective, to reconsider the interests of Europe's populace rather than solely focusing on national self-interests.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the supporters of the Treaty of Lisbon have emphasised the fact that it does away with the democratic deficit in the EU. In reality, the first year has shown that the European Parliament has had to fight with the Council and the Commission simply to achieve parliamentary codecision and democratic control. The codecision of Parliament on all budgetary questions is a democratic minimum standard. I repeat: it is a minimum standard. This Parliament was elected to bring the European ideal to life and to act as a counterbalance to national egotism. It is our duty not to compromise which it comes to the involvement of the European Parliament in the negotiations concerning the financial framework for the 2013 to 2020 budgets. New tasks cannot be financed by budget reallocations, or at least only to a very limited extent. When faced by the tight budgets in the Member States, it is completely incomprehensible that the Council is refusing to discuss the own resources with Parliament. The European budget must be a concrete representation of the solidarity of the Member States. Otherwise, we will move away from the fundamental idea of European integration and lose the respect of the citizens of Europe. The net contributors seem to have forgotten this. While their companies are making profits on the European internal market, Germany and other countries are being thoroughly miserly when it comes to overcoming the joint challenges faced by Europe. Solidarity has been redefined and one Member State will only help the others when this is to its own advantage. The German banks are so deeply enmeshed in the crisis in the Irish financial sector that the lifeline is just as important for the rescuers as it is for those being rescued. In recent years, my group has rejected the EU budget. We believe that its priorities are wrong and that too little attention has been paid to overcoming the decline in environmental and social prosperity. This has deepened the social divide within the EU and within the Member States. It is totally unacceptable for major projects such as ITER to be financed at the expense of projects which provide direct funding for students, the unemployed, rural areas and local communities. To summarise, it is high time, and I admit this is a somewhat simplistic way of putting it, for us to think more closely about the people of Europe in European terms and not in egotistic national terms.

I hereby express my enthusiasm regarding the presented report, emphasizing its endorsement of alternative dispute resolutions, particularly those applicable to both consumer and family law contexts. Mediation, among these alternatives, offers outcomes comparable to or even superior to traditional litigation procedures. In light of significant challenges within Ireland's family law framework, including pervasive instances of parental alienating tactics, there is compelling evidence supporting the adoption of accessible, efficacious, and robust mediation services. My nation boasts high legal fees, frequently cited as an impediment to access to justice across the European Union. Consequently, alternative dispute mechanisms often serve as the sole viable recourse for many citizens. Therefore, I am gratified to observe this initiative prominently featured in the report. It represents a vital step towards enhancing accessibility and effectiveness in resolving disputes through non-judicial channels.

Mr President, I have to say, I really welcome this report because I firmly believe that alternative dispute resolution processes should be used as widely as possible, whether it’s consumer law or family law. Things like mediation get results that are at least as good, if not actually better than anything that would come out of a court process. And given the huge issues facing, for example, Ireland’s family law system, including the massive problems with concepts such as parental alienation creeping into it and being put forward by court experts who aren’t experts, it really would be better if we were to support available and robust and effective mediation processes. I come from a country with some of the highest legal costs in Europe. They are at levels which have been criticised consistently in the EU rule of law reports as being a barrier to justice. That means that alternative dispute resolution is often people’s only option and if that’s not available, then they’re out of options. So I’m really glad to see it being put centre stage in this report.
